The ward's historical Labour dominance (winning every regular election 2021–2024, often with over 1,000 votes and 60–80%+ share) shows there is a meaningful Labour base, and Cardwell is the same candidate who came very close in 2025 and is now effectively the incumbent challenger. In 2026 this is a regular annual election rather than a by-election; regular elections typically see higher turnout (c.25-28% rather than 22%), which historically has benefited Labour in this ward. However, the national swing toward Reform has been large enough that the higher-turnout effect may be offset. Conservatives (Grandi) have a persistent but weak base of ~17% that has now likely eroded further to Reform, giving him only a small vote share. Green and TUSC are fringe candidates here with low historical vote shares. Overall, Topp is the frontrunner but the race is genuinely competitive, with Cardwell a credible second.

Ward history shows Labour won in 2022, 2023, and 2024 (three consecutive wins), giving them a structural advantage in this urban Lincoln ward. In 2024 Labour polled 45.4%, Conservative 32.6%, Reform 10.6%, Green 6.8%, LD 4.4%. Key factors for 2026: (1) Labour structural advantage: Three consecutive wins and a strong urban core make Labour the baseline favourite, despite Funmi Adeyemi's weak personal prior record in other (more rural/suburban) seats. (2) Reform surge: Liam Kelly is a strong candidate—he won Swallow Beck & Witham County Council division in May 2025 with 40.71%, demonstrating he can win locally in Lincolnshire. Nationally, Reform has been polling 20–30% and performing very well in 2025 local elections. Reform's vote in Moorland was only 10.6% in 2024 and likely jumps significantly in 2026. (3) Conservative decline: Matthew Fido has a personal vote (won 2021 with 47.9%) but fell sharply to 32.6% in 2024. He retains name recognition and community engagement, but Conservative support nationally continues to erode. (4) Right-of-centre vote split: The combined Conservative+Reform vote exceeded Labour's share even in 2024 (~43.2% vs 45.4%), but split between two parties it helps Labour survive. Projected rough 2026 shares: Labour ~37–40%, Reform ~23–26%, Conservative ~24–27%, Green ~6%, LD ~5%. Under most scenarios Labour leads, but the contest is genuinely uncertain. Green (Murray consistent ~5% in Lincoln) and LD (Spires no record) are long shots. The main uncertainty is whether Reform's surge is large enough to overcome Labour's lead, or whether Fido's personal vote in this specific ward keeps Conservatives competitive for second. Overall, Labour remains the most likely winner but with substantial probability of an upset from Reform or Conservative.

Prettygate ward has been a Conservative stronghold across all four data points (2021–2024), but the 2026 contest presents several structural changes that narrow the gap considerably.

**Historical baseline:** The Conservatives have won every annual election in this ward. However, the margins have varied sharply depending on incumbency: Roger Buston (the councillor whose seat is up in 2026) won in 2022 with 43.6% vs Loxley's 32.5% — an 11pp gap. In 2023 without a newly elected Buston incumbent, the result was Con 42.0% vs Lib Dem 41.3% — virtually tied. Sue Lissimore (not up this year) won 50.7% in 2024, suggesting strong personal vote effects. The 2026 seat is Buston's, and Buston is not defending it.

**New Conservative candidate:** David Linghorn-Baker has been active in the ward for ~5 years (community work, now a post office worker in Prettygate) and brings genuine local engagement. However, his three prior candidacies in other wards all resulted in losses and relatively modest vote shares (33%, 16%, 17%). He lacks the incumbency advantage that previous Conservative winners had, and is running against a well-established local campaigner in Loxley.

**Reform UK entry:** Sophie Preston-Hall adds a new dimension. Her prior results (6.2% in 2023, 12.0% in 2024 in Mersea & Pyefleet) understate Reform's likely 2026 ceiling, given the national surge of the party heading into May 2026 elections. A Reform share of roughly 15%+ would draw disproportionately from the Conservative base, compressing Linghorn-Baker's vote and making the LD–Con race closer, potentially flipping the lead.

**John Loxley (LD):** Has stood here every year since 2021, building from 10% to 34% to 41% to 32% (with Lissimore's strong incumbency depressing his 2024 share). He has strong local roots and benefits from national Lib Dem momentum in southern English seats. The combination of no Conservative incumbent, Reform splitting the right-of-centre vote, and his persistent local campaigning makes him a slight favourite.

**Labour and Greens:** Chris Coates got 13% in 2024; with Reform drawing from the protest vote and Labour nationally under pressure in local contests, his share is likely to shrink. Stella Eldon (Green) is an entirely new candidate with no profile, likely 3–5%.

**Conclusion:** This is a genuinely competitive two-horse race between Loxley (LD) and Linghorn-Baker (Con), with Reform adding substantial uncertainty. The Lib Dem edges ahead as slight favourite due to Loxley's established local presence, the loss of Conservative incumbency, and the vote-splitting effect of Reform on the Conservative right flank in what is historically a competitive ward when incumbency is controlled for.

The most predictive data point is the May 2025 by-election held in this exact ward, where Anthony John Payne (Reform UK, candidate 118901) won with 34.83% of the vote, defeating Labour's candidate (27.82%), an Independent (11.44%), Green (11.08%), Conservative (8.35%), and Lib Dem (6.49%). Payne is now the incumbent, which provides a structural advantage.

Key factors for 2026:
1. INCUMBENCY: Payne won the most recent contest in this ward and enjoys name recognition and incumbency benefits.
2. NO INDEPENDENT: The 2025 by-election had an independent candidate who took 11.44% of the vote (259 votes). In 2026, no independent is running. These votes will redistribute. Some may go to Labour, but others to Reform or Green, likely not dramatically shifting the result.
3. LABOUR CANDIDATE WEAKNESS: Steve Casemore (102891) has a poor personal electoral record — 6.11% in Bath/NE Somerset 2023 and only 2.15% in Devon 2025 — suggesting limited local campaigning effectiveness. The 2025 Labour candidate (Paula Black) achieved 27.82%; Casemore seems likely to underperform her.
4. CONSERVATIVE SLIGHT RECOVERY POSSIBLE: David Thompson (11130) achieved 23.84% in Pinhoe 2024, considerably stronger than the 8.35% the Conservative candidate received in the 2025 by-election here. However, the long-term trend in this ward for Conservatives is strongly negative (21.7% → 18.1% → 13.2% → 8.4%), and some potential Conservative voters may have already drifted to Reform UK.
5. GREEN CONSISTENT BUT NOT COMPETITIVE: Martin Ayres (120541) got 11.08% in the 2025 by-election and 11% in the overlapping Devon county division. He is a consistent third/fourth-place finisher but not a winner.
6. LIB DEM PERENNIALLY WEAK: Michael Payne (11175) has never exceeded 9.56% in this ward across three attempts; Lib Dems are clearly not competitive here.
7. NATIONAL CONTEXT: As of May 2026, Reform UK has maintained strong momentum nationally since the 2025 local elections. Labour has been in government since mid-2024 and faces mid-term difficulties.

Overall, Reform UK remains the clear favourite with incumbency and national momentum. Labour is the main challenger but faces candidate quality disadvantage. The gap between them (7 points in 2025) is unlikely to close enough for a Labour upset, but it's not impossible, especially with independent votes being freed up. Conservative could recover to double digits but not enough to win. Green and Lib Dem are not competitive for the seat.

The key piece of evidence is the 2025 by-election held on the regular May 2025 local election day: Amanda Napper (Reform UK) won Throston with 58.3% (889 votes) against Labour (31.8%, 486) and Conservative (9.9%, 151). This was not a low-turnout protest by-election in isolation — it was held on the same day as other May 2025 local elections, making it a strong signal of real ward preferences. Napper is now the incumbent councillor, a significant structural advantage in local elections.

Historical ward context: from 2022–2024 Throston was a safe Labour seat (Labour winning 717, 765, and 1015 votes in successive contests). The 2025 swing to Reform UK was dramatic and in line with Reform UK's nationwide surge in the May 2025 local elections. By May 2026, national Reform UK polling has remained strong with no clear evidence of a major reversal.

For 2026, there are now five candidates (Labour, Reform, Conservative, Lib Dem, Green) vs. three in 2025. The addition of Lib Dem and Green candidates is likely to draw marginally from Labour and Conservative, making it harder for the anti-Reform vote to consolidate. Callaghan (Labour) has no prior electoral record, while Napper is an established local name and incumbent.

Risks to Reform UK holding: higher turnout in a regular full-council election year could partially recover Labour's vote; some mean-reversion from a high-water 58% figure is possible; a coordinated opposition could squeeze the race. However, even a 10-15 point drop from 58% would still leave Reform well clear of Labour. The opposition would need an enormous coordinated swing — unlikely in a five-candidate race. Lyall (Conservative) has performed weakly in prior Hartlepool elections (15–18%) and brings no Throston history. Lib Dem and Green candidates have zero prior records and Throston is not a natural territory for either party.

Weighting all these factors: Napper (Reform) is a heavy favourite at ~80%, Labour retains meaningful but minority upside at ~13%, Conservatives have marginal hope at ~4%, and Lib Dems/Greens are long shots at ~2% and ~1% respectively.

Killingbeck & Seacroft is a safe Labour ward in urban east Leeds. Labour has won every cycle from 2021–2024 with vote shares consistently above 59–62%. In 2022, the incumbent Labour candidate John Tudor (now standing again) won with 61.93% of the vote. No other candidate has exceeded 24% in any of the four most recent contests, and by 2024 the next-best finisher (Gaskell, Conservative) received only 11.55%. Reform UK got 10.1% in 2024 under a different candidate; their 2026 candidate (Dresser) has broader local experience but still faces an enormous structural deficit. The Conservatives have trended sharply downward in this ward (from ~24% in 2021 to ~11% in 2024). Greens, Lib Dems, and SDP are all fringe-level performers here. Even accounting for plausible Labour national headwinds in 2026 (government mid-term drag, Reform UK's national growth), the arithmetic gap Labour holds is too large to be overcome: for any challenger to win, Labour would need to roughly halve its vote while one challenger doubled or tripled theirs simultaneously. Tudor's personal recognition from his 2022 win in this specific ward adds further incumbency advantage. The dominant scenario remains a comfortable Labour hold; Reform UK is assigned a small non-trivial probability as the most credible challenger given national trends, but the probability of an upset remains low.

Bowthorpe ward analysis as of 2026-05-01:

**Historical baseline:** Labour has won every regular Bowthorpe election from 2021–2024, consistently polling 1,170–1,201 votes. The ward appeared safe Labour until the May 2025 by-election.

**2025 by-election (the pivotal data point):** Labour (689) beat Reform UK's Nick Taylor (688) by a single vote, with Greens (430), Conservatives (281), and Lib Dems (102) trailing. Total turnout (~2,190) was comparable to regular election totals (1,941–2,261), so this is not a low-engagement by-election anomaly — it reflects genuine competitive dynamics. Reform UK surged from nowhere to near-parity with Labour in one cycle.

**Labour (Mike Sands, 138569):** Strong structural advantages — he won Bowthorpe in 2022 with 1,170 votes and is a known local figure. Labour incumbency in the ward since the by-election win adds marginal benefit. In regular elections, Labour's base is more likely to turn out fully. However, Labour nationally is unpopular and the 2025 result showed the ward is no longer safe.

**Reform UK (Nick Taylor, 113142):** Came within 1 vote in 2025 with the same candidate, who now has strong local name recognition. National Reform UK momentum in 2025–2026 is a tailwind. His statement reflects an appeal to anti-establishment and former Conservative voters, which is consistent with the collapse of the Conservative vote in the ward (from 662 in 2021 to 281 in 2025). There is a real path to victory if he converts more soft Labour or Conservative voters.

**Green (Amber Smith, 138571):** Green performed exceptionally in 2025 (430 vs. ~220–226 in regular elections). The 2026 candidate is new with no prior record. Likely some regression toward regular-election Green levels (~200–300), with some of the 2025 surge being a by-election protest effect. A long-shot winner but not negligible.

**Conservative (Rebeka Jones, 112676):** Previously ran in Bowthorpe in 2024 (17.6%, 342 votes), but Conservatives have collapsed to 281 votes in 2025. In an environment favoring Reform UK, former Conservative voters are likely continuing to migrate to Reform. Very low win probability.

**Lib Dem (Neil Hardman, 41089):** Weak prior performances (6.79% in a different ward in 2021, 3.11% in another ward in 2024). Historical Bowthorpe Lib Dem baseline is around 100–240 votes, always distant. Near-negligible win probability.

**Verdict:** This is essentially a two-horse race between Labour and Reform UK, virtually indistinguishable based on the 2025 result. Labour retains a slight edge due to its stronger organizational base in regular elections and the fact that Mike Sands previously won this exact ward. Reform UK is a genuine threat and could flip the seat if national momentum continues and Green voters return to their lower baseline.

Ward history and candidate profiles drive this forecast. Brierfield East & Clover Hill has been won by Labour or ex-Labour Independents in every cycle since 2021. The ward history (heavy South Asian community participation, Labour dominating 2021 3-seat election, Ahmed winning 2022 for Labour, Ashraf winning 2024 as ex-Labour Independent with no Labour opposition) points to a ward with a strong community-oriented electorate. Key factors by candidate: (1) Sajjad Ahmed (Independent, 90809) is the strongest contender: he is the sitting councillor who won this exact seat in 2022 (48.27%), has personal incumbency advantage and community credibility, and follows the same pattern as Ashraf (ex-Labour Independent) winning in 2024. The main risk is that Labour is now fielding a candidate to split his traditional base. (2) Labour (Walsh, 133290) benefits from the party's historically strong brand in this ward (all 3 seats in 2021, competitive in 2022), but Walsh has no electoral record, no apparent local profile, and must compete against a well-known incumbent. National Labour fatigue under Starmer (welfare cuts, governing difficulties by 2026) also erodes the party label. (3) Reform UK (Metcalfe, 1312) has strong regional momentum — 32.38% in the adjacent Pendle Hill division in May 2025 — and national polling peaks around 2026. However, the ward's demonstrated electoral history shows an overwhelmingly community-oriented (South Asian) electorate where Reform's immigration and cultural platform is markedly less appealing. Reform's realistic ceiling is constrained by demographics, though they can attract white working-class anti-establishment voters previously going Conservative. (4) Conservatives (McCormick, 58098) have collapsed from ~45% in 2022 to ~17% in the ward by 2024; McCormick has never won in her multiple Brierfield attempts and lacks the community-candidate appeal that made Conservative competition viable in 2022. (5) Green and Liberal Democrat candidates have negligible histories in this ward type and are not competitive. The most likely outcome is an Ahmed victory, with Labour and Reform as credible but secondary threats, and Conservatives playing a spoiler role.

Historical record: Labour has won Sutton & Mount Gould in each of the four prior recorded contests (2021–2024) with vote shares consistently in the 55–58% range. The Labour candidate (Mary Aspinall) is an incumbent, having won this seat in 2022 with 57.1%. This is a very high baseline to overcome.

Key swing factors for 2026: Reform UK entered this ward only in 2024 at 8.3% but has been surging nationally. If Reform roughly triples its 2024 local showing (consistent with strong national polling), it might reach ~20–25%. The Green candidate's statement (dated 29 April 2026, very close to the election) explicitly claims 'the race in Sutton & Mount Gould is close between the Greens and Reform,' suggesting Labour may have slipped substantially. However, this is also campaign rhetoric designed to attract tactical votes, and all Green candidates have an incentive to claim competitiveness.

For Labour to lose, its vote would need to fall from ~56% to below ~30%, a drop of 25+ percentage points — very unusual even in catastrophic years for a party, particularly for a ward with strong Labour roots and a local incumbent. Even generous assumptions about a Labour collapse (down 15–20 points) would still leave Labour ahead if the opposition vote is split among Reform, Green, and Conservative.

Conservative (Zajko): No prior record; Conservative vote has collapsed from 33% in 2021 to 15% in 2024. Minor factor in 2026.
Lib Dem (Ball): Consistently under 7% in this ward; no realistic path to victory.
TUSC (Sampson): Historical ceiling ~3%; negligible.

Conclusion: Labour remains the strong favourite given the historical dominance and incumbency advantage, but there is meaningful uncertainty due to the national Reform surge and the Green candidate's specific claims about competitiveness. Reform UK and Greens each carry real but smaller probabilities.

Fratton is a genuinely competitive three-way ward. Recent history: Lib Dems won in 2021, Labour (Coles) won in 2022, Lib Dems won in 2023 and 2024. The 2024 result was very tight: LD 31.7%, PIP (Short) 28.0%, Labour 28.0%, Con 8%, Green 4.3%.

Key dynamics for 2026:

1. Labour (Tom Coles, 46260): Coles won Fratton with 43.5% in 2022 — the only time he has stood here he won convincingly. Labour finished a weak third in 2024 under a different candidate, so his personal vote returning is significant. However, Labour is struggling nationally by May 2026 and the Reform threat has grown considerably since 2022. Estimated 33% win probability.

2. Reform UK (Jacob Short, 102289): Short has built a strong local base — 16.6% in 2023 and 28.0% in 2024 as Portsmouth Independent. Switching to the Reform UK brand adds national momentum and branding, though his statement still references 'Portsmouth Independents' suggesting a somewhat independent appeal. Reform UK has been polling very strongly in English local elections heading into 2026, and Fratton is a working-class ward where Reform tends to overperform. Estimated 32% win probability.

3. Lib Dems (Billy Ansell, 131105): The Lib Dems have won three of the last four Fratton elections and currently hold two of the three seats (Brown and Ashmore are both Lib Dem). Party machinery and incumbency effects are real. However, Ansell is a new, untested candidate replacing proven winners. When Lib Dems ran a non-incumbent in 2022 they narrowly lost to Coles. Estimated 29% win probability.

4. Conservatives (Tim Edwards, 112298): Got only 8% in 2024. Very unlikely to win. Estimated 4%.

5. Greens (Tim Sheerman-Chase, 38014): Has contested Copnor (not Fratton) recently, with declining vote shares (10.6% → 8.9% → 4.8%). Greens have been 4–5% in Fratton historically. No realistic path to victory. Estimated 2%.

New Bilton has been a consistent Labour hold since at least 2021, with Labour winning every regular and by-election in the ward history. However, the May 2025 by-election showed a dramatic shift: Angela Mary Thompson (Labour) won with only 34.3% versus Reform UK's 30.2% — a margin of just 56 votes. This is the single most important piece of evidence for 2026.

Key factors favouring Labour (Thompson): (1) Incumbency — Thompson won here just 12 months ago and is the sitting councillor; (2) Ward history — Labour has polled 53–62% in regular elections 2021–2024; (3) Regular elections tend to have better Labour turnout mobilisation than by-elections, potentially widening their lead slightly over the by-election result; (4) The Labour 'floor' in this ward appears to be around 34% even in a difficult by-election environment.

Key factors favouring Reform (Sonko): (1) Sonko is a proven winner — she was elected to Warwickshire County Council's Earl Craven seat in May 2025 with 33.7%, showing she can win in this region; (2) Reform came within ~4 percentage points in the 2025 by-election with a different, less proven candidate; (3) The Conservative vote in New Bilton has collapsed from 36% (2021) to 17.7% (2025 by-election) — some of those votes may flow to Reform rather than returning to Conservatives, putting ceiling pressure on Labour.

Conservatives: No prior record for Bearne; the party's trajectory in this ward is severely downward. Winning probability very low but they retain some structural vote.

Greens and Lib Dems: Both polling in single digits historically in this ward; no realistic path to victory.

Overall: Labour is the modest favourite due to incumbency and ward DNA, but Reform poses a genuine threat given the near-miss in 2025 and Sonko's demonstrated local electoral success. This is too close to assign high confidence to either frontrunner.

Eccles ward is a deeply entrenched Labour stronghold. In every single-seat election held here (2022, 2023, 2024), the Labour candidate has won with around 60% of the vote, compared to roughly 20-25% for the second-place Conservative and single-digit shares for everyone else. The Labour vote has remained remarkably stable in absolute terms (~1,964–2,121 votes), and the ward has no historical precedent of a close contest.

Nathaniel Tetteh is the incumbent councillor returning to contest the same seat he won in 2022 (60.17%), with a detailed candidate statement and active community engagement. His prior record gives him a strong incumbency advantage.

The main source of uncertainty is Reform UK (Denis Adshead), which is entering the race without a prior local record here. Reform made significant gains across England in 2025 and continues to poll strongly nationally (~20%+). In working-class northern English seats, Reform can draw from both disaffected Labour and former Conservative voters. However, Eccles presents a very high bar: even if Reform consolidates the entire non-Green, non-Lib Dem opposition vote, Labour's structural advantage of ~2,100 votes versus ~565 for the nearest rival in 2024 leaves an enormous gap to bridge.

The Conservative vote is likely to be further depressed by Reform's presence, and Greens may modestly grow from their 479 in 2024. Lib Dems and TUSC remain marginal. None of these dynamics are plausible pathways to victory against Labour given the historical margin.

I assign Labour (Tetteh) ~87% probability of winning, with a small but non-trivial ~6% for Reform UK reflecting the possibility of a more dramatic national swing than baseline, ~3% for Green continuing their upward trajectory into a surprise result, and residual shares for Conservative, Lib Dem, and TUSC.

Crookes & Crosspool is a three-seat ward with seats contested in rotation. The 2026 contest is for the seat Minesh Parekh (Labour) won in 2022. The ward has been a consistent Labour–Lib Dem battleground: Labour won in 2021 (38.9%), Labour won in 2022 (37.5% vs Lib Dem 34.5%), Lib Dems won in 2023 (41.0% vs Labour 38.2%), and Labour won the 2024 rotation (49.4% vs Lib Dem 32.7%). The 2024 Labour result was unusually strong, likely reflecting general election momentum; the underlying baseline for this specific seat (last contested in 2022) shows a razor-thin Labour lead over the Lib Dems. Parekh benefits from incumbency and a stated local record; Jordan Barry (Lib Dem) carries name recognition from a 2188-vote (31.9%) performance in this exact ward in 2024 and operates in a party that has been surging in suburban/urban English councils. The Green candidate (Lewis-Creser) ran credibly in Walkley in 2024 (32%) but Greens have historically capped at 14–20% in Crookes & Crosspool; a Green squeeze or surge to third is plausible but winning is unlikely. Reform UK is new to this ward; the educated, progressive character of the ward limits their ceiling substantially compared to their national surge. Conservatives and TUSC are also-rans. The main contest is Labour vs Lib Dems, with Labour as a modest favourite due to incumbency and the ward's recent Labour majority on the council, but genuine uncertainty warranting roughly 50/33 Labour/Lib Dem split.

Key evidence: Labour (Asma Suleman) won every regular Holywell election 2021–2024 with vote shares of 53–63%, and narrowly won the May 2025 by-election at 33.2% vs Lib Dems at 30.8%, Reform UK at 17%, Conservatives at 10.4%, Greens at 7.3%, TUSC at 1.3%. The by-election was the most recent and directly relevant data point. Labour carries incumbency advantage: Suleman won the by-election and is a known quantity in the ward. However, the by-election revealed real vulnerability — a margin of only 41 votes over the Lib Dems — reflecting both Reform UK fragmenting the right-of-centre vote and the Lib Dems' ability to attract tactical votes. For the 2026 regular election: (1) Higher turnout in regular May elections (24–31% historically) typically advantages Labour compared to lower-turnout by-elections. (2) The Lib Dem candidate (Emil Rowe) has no prior electoral record, which is a disadvantage compared to the by-election Lib Dem challenger who nearly won. (3) Reform UK (Michael Barrett) ran in the Oxhey by-election at 17.4% and a different Reform candidate got 17% in Holywell in 2025; national Reform momentum in 2026 could sustain or grow this share. (4) The Conservative collapse (from ~20% in 2022–23 to ~10% in 2025) is entrenched; Sanjaya Pant has run multiple times without winning. (5) By-elections often show exaggerated swings that partially reverse at regular elections. Labour is assigned the highest probability given incumbent status, historical strength, and likely turnout advantage, but with meaningful probability assigned to Lib Dems given their near-win in 2025 and Reform UK given national trends. Conservatives, Greens, and TUSC have only marginal win probabilities.

Peartree ward has returned a Liberal Democrat councillor at every election from 2021 through the May 2025 by-election (five consecutive wins). Duncan Merlin Jones (candidate 93637) is the 2022 incumbent, won with a commanding 51.78% vote share, has a detailed candidate statement reflecting deep local engagement, and served as Cabinet member for Resources – all factors that bolster an incumbency advantage.

However, the May 2025 by-election introduced a major complication: Reform UK (Mark Biddle) came within 45 votes of the Liberal Democrats (375 vs 420, a gap of ~3.5 pp), displacing Labour into a distant third. This suggests genuine vulnerability. The key questions for 2026 are: (1) whether Reform's by-election surge was a protest-vote spike inflated by low turnout (22%) or a durable realignment; and (2) whether Labour partially recovers its historically second-place position.

Factors favouring Lib Dem win: higher expected turnout in a regular cycle (~24-28%) typically benefits incumbents and organised parties over protest surges; Lib Dem GOTV machinery in a held ward; well-known local candidate vs. Reform's Ian Colpitts who has no prior local electoral record; by-election dynamics (low turnout, no other major elections on the day) overstate Reform's ceiling.

Factors creating uncertainty: Reform UK momentum has been sustained nationally as of spring 2026 under a Labour government facing headwinds; the 2025 result demonstrated the ward can be competitive; Labour weakness (Darren Lewis took only 13.54% in the simultaneous 2025 county council contest in the same area) means Lib Dems cannot rely on Labour splitting the Reform vote.

Conservatives have collapsed from 23.5% in 2021 to 8% in 2025 and have a new, unknown candidate. Greens have been stable at ~7-8% but have no realistic path to victory.

On balance, the Lib Dems remain clear favourites given five consecutive wins, a credentialed incumbent, and regular-election turnout effects, but Reform UK retains a meaningful probability reflecting the near-miss of 2025.

Central Meon Valley has returned a Green councillor in every election since 2022, with vote shares of 55% (2022), 63% (2023), and 55% (2024). Malcolm Wallace (Green, 72025) is the sitting councillor for this ward (elected 2022) and also won the adjacent Hampshire County Council Meon Valley seat in a 2024 by-election (35.8%). He has deep local roots (20+ years in the Meon Valley), a strong personal vote, and an established record combatting over-development and protecting local waterways. All of this makes him the overwhelming favourite. The Conservatives (42402, Newberry) have been declining from ~43% in 2021 to ~26-31% in 2022-2024; they have no realistic path to overtaking the Greens under current conditions. The Liberal Democrats (17815, Bentote) showed some growth in 2024 (19%) and Bentote has Winchester District Council experience in adjacent wards, but they remain well behind the Greens. Reform UK (8757, Baxter) has no local electoral record, but the national Reform surge in early 2026 introduces meaningful uncertainty; they are most likely to cannibalise Conservative votes, further reducing Con prospects without threatening the Green hold. In a rural Hampshire village ward, Reform's ceiling is lower than in post-industrial or coastal seats. Labour (6148, Davis) has never exceeded 6% in this ward and is essentially a spoiler. The main uncertainty is the national-swing wildcard from Reform, and the possibility that Lib Dem growth plus Con collapse creates a closer race than the historical margins suggest, but none of these scenarios is likely to displace Wallace.

Buckingham has been highly volatile: Conservative (Boram) won comfortably in 2021 (49.7%) and Conservative held again narrowly in 2022, but Labour (Jenner) won decisively in 2024 with 50.9% vs Boram's reduced 31.7% share, a ~19-point swing. That gives the Labour Party a strong incumbency baseline heading into 2026, even though the 2026 Labour candidate (Devoy) is untested and running without the 'Co-operative' branding used in 2024. Kevin Boram, the pre-2024 long-serving Conservative councillor for this ward, is attempting a comeback and retains local name recognition, giving Conservatives a real but secondary chance, especially if some 2024 Labour swing was cyclical/national-mood driven and could partially revert. The introduction of a Reform UK candidate (no prior record) adds a new competitor likely to draw disproportionately from the Conservative-leaning protest vote, which both hurts Boram's recovery chances and creates a plausible, if less likely, path for Reform to win outright if anti-incumbent sentiment is strong and splits are favorable. The Green candidate, Gabrielle Crisp, is notable as a sitting councillor relocating from a much stronger Green ward (St Nicolas, 46.8% in 2021), which may add some personal-vote credibility, but Buckingham's own Green history is weak (5-11% range), so an upset here is unlikely though not negligible given her incumbency elsewhere and strong community-focused statement. The Liberal Democrat candidate (Harpley) previously scored only 3% in a different ward in 2024, well below the 12% the LibDems achieved in Buckingham that year, suggesting a weaker personal vote and making a Lib Dem win very unlikely. Overall, Labour is treated as the moderate favorite to retain on the strength of its 2024 result and incumbency, with Conservative as the leading challenger, Reform as a credible but lower-probability spoiler/contender, and Green and Lib Dem as long-shot outcomes given ward-specific historical weakness despite candidate-level differences.'

This ward has trended Labour in three straight annual elections (2022 Lee 961 vs Con 904; 2023 Freeman 886 vs Con 527; 2024 West 706 vs Con Compton-Burnett 690), with the race tightening dramatically to just a 16-vote margin in 2024. That suggests a genuinely marginal, Labour-leaning seat rather than a safe one. Two major complicating factors for 2026: (1) Labour's 2024 winner, Zander West, is not on this ballot; the new Labour candidate, Cait Fuller, is a transplant from Kempshott & Buckskin where she polled a credible 28.9% in 2024, giving her some campaigning competence but no local incumbency advantage here. (2) The centre-right/anti-Labour vote is now split three ways: the official Conservative nominee Mike Patchett (a perennial also-ran who was the weakest of three Tory candidates in the 2021 all-out contest), the former sitting Conservative councillor Abigail Compton-Burnett who nearly won in 2024 (690 votes, 16 short) but is now standing as an Independent, and Reform UK's Christina Jordan, who has only modest previous local vote share (8% in a different ward) but represents a new insurgent option that could pull further from the Tory-leaning base. This fragmentation makes it hard for either right-of-Labour candidate to consolidate the vote that nearly won in 2024, favoring Labour's Fuller as the most likely winner, though not overwhelmingly given she lacks personal incumbency and the ward's razor-thin recent margins. Compton-Burnett retains meaningful personal/local-recognition upside as a near-winner and former councillor, giving her a real if secondary chance, especially if disaffected Conservatives coalesce around her rather than the official party candidate. Patchett, McKay (perennial Lib Dem candidate polling 3-7%), and Farrington (Green, polling 5-8% in a different ward) are given smaller, longshot probabilities reflecting weak underlying vote-share histories and the crowded splitting of the non-Labour vote, while Jordan for Reform is given a modest chance reflecting Reform's national momentum but limited specific evidence of ward-level strength.

West Chesterton has been reliably Labour-held at the single-seat level from 2022-2024 (Labour vote share 42-43%, comfortably ahead of second-placed Lib Dems on ~33-36%), but the most recent contest in the ward - the May 2025 by-election - saw a clear swing: Lib Dem candidate Jamie Dalzell won (38.5%) over Labour (33.0%), with Green up to 17.1% and Reform entering at 6.3%, continuing multi-cycle trends of rising Lib Dem/Green shares and falling Conservative shares. This is the single most recent and informative ward-level data point and shows Labour is genuinely vulnerable. However, two important candidate-quality factors cut back toward Labour for 2026: (1) Labour's candidate, Richard Swift, is the incumbent councillor for this specific seat (elected here in 2022) and previously contested the ward in 2021, giving him real personal recognition and an incumbency/experience advantage that the unknown 2025 by-election Labour candidate (Rosy Greenlees) lacked; (2) the Lib Dem candidate this time, Guy Mills, is a first-time candidate with no electoral record, in sharp contrast to Jamie Dalzell, who had built support across three prior contests (2021, 2022, 2023-24 near-misses) before finally winning in 2025 - his personal vote does not transfer to Mills. These offsetting effects (adverse national/local trend for Labour vs. favorable candidate-quality gap for Labour) make this close to a genuine toss-up between Labour and the Lib Dems, with Labour treated as a modest favorite mainly due to incumbency. The Green candidate, Hannah Copley, is an experienced former councillor (elected in Abbey 2021) whose local vote share has been steadily rising (11.7% to 17.4% over recent cycles) and provides a credible but still clearly third-place threat rather than a likely winner. Reform UK's local entrant (Geoff Leach) benefits from a plausible national polling surge by 2026 but starts from a low local base (6.3% in the most recent contest) in a Remain-leaning university city, capping realistic upside. The Conservative candidate, Mike Harford, has repeatedly polled in the single digits across six prior contests in this ward and shows no sign of a breakthrough, while the independent candidate has no track record and no obvious base. Overall probabilities reflect a close Labour/Lib Dem contest, a meaningful but clearly third-place Green prospect, and negligible chances for Reform, Conservative, and the independent.

Deddington has been a reliably Conservative ward across every contest from 2021 through the 2025 by-election, with Conservative candidates winning comfortably each time (typically 44-48% of the vote) against a fragmented opposition. The 2026 Conservative candidate, Eddie Fraser Reeves, previously won this exact seat outright in 2022 (45.9%), giving him a strong personal track record in this specific ward, which is a meaningful positive signal beyond generic incumbency. However, the introduction of a Reform UK candidate (Gail Collingwood-Turner) with no prior electoral record represents a significant new variable: Reform has emerged as a serious electoral force in recent English local elections, often drawing disproportionately from Conservative-leaning voters in similar semi-rural, small-town wards, and can plausibly split or overtake the traditional Tory vote share where no historical local baseline yet exists. Absent ward-specific polling on Reform's strength, I treat this as a substantial but not dominant risk to the Conservative incumbency advantage. Labour's Annette Murphy has run in this ward three times with a rising vote share (22%->27%->33% in 2022-2024), suggesting some residual strength, though her 2025 non-participation and the collapse of the Labour by-election candidate to 14% raises doubts about how much of that growth was candidate-specific momentum versus anti-Conservative tactical consolidation that may now fragment further with Reform's entry. James Hartley (Lib Dem) has a declining vote share trend in this ward (20.75%->18.71%->12.58%) even though a different Lib Dem candidate surged to 27.6% in the 2025 by-election, suggesting Lib Dem support here is more anti-Conservative protest vote than personal Hartley support, capping his likely ceiling. Aaron Bliss (Green) has a very consistent but low ~10-11% vote share across five elections, making a win highly unlikely. Overall, the Conservative candidate remains the most likely winner given his specific ward-level electoral history and the fragmentation of opposition across four other parties, but a meaningful probability is assigned to Reform UK given the general national rise of that party and the total absence of any historical benchmark to constrain their potential ceiling in this contest, with smaller residual chances for Labour and the Liberal Democrats reflecting their track records of second/third-place finishes, and a minimal chance for the Green candidate given a consistent but non-competitive vote share.

This ward was a safe Labour seat for years (2021-2024 results show Labour winning comfortably with 900-1200+ votes vs. a distant Conservative second on 250-400 votes; TUSC and Green were minor fringe players). That changed dramatically in the May 2025 by-election, when Reform UK's Martin Topp won a narrow victory (672 votes, 40.1%) over Labour's Ian Cardwell (619 votes, 36.9%), a swing of roughly 53 votes/3 points, while the Conservative vote collapsed to under 10%. Crucially, this is a like-for-like rematch: the same two lead candidates (Topp for Reform, Cardwell for Labour) are contesting again, giving strong signal about the two-horse nature of this race. Topp's by-election win is corroborated by his concurrent 2025 win to the overlapping Lancashire County Council 'Chorley North' division with an even larger margin (41.1% vs a fragmented field), suggesting the pro-Reform shift in this locality is not a one-off protest vote but reflects a real and possibly consolidating base, aided by incumbency and the broader national trend of Reform UK's sustained strength through 2025-26 in northern England, ex-Labour-leaning wards. Against that, this is now a scheduled all-out local round (not a single-seat by-election), which typically brings higher and more 'normal' turnout that could partially favor Labour's traditional get-out-the-vote strength and let disaffected 2025 protest voters drift back. Labour also retains a plausible path back given the closeness of the prior result and the ward's underlying demographic lean. Conservative, Green, and TUSC remain marginal: Conservative vote share has been declining (17%->17%->9.5% by-election) and is unlikely to be competitive; Green and TUSC have consistently polled in single digits and show no signs of a breakthrough. Overall I model this as a close, primarily two-way Reform-vs-Labour contest with a modest edge to the Reform UK incumbent given momentum, incumbency, and corroborating county-level results, while leaving Labour a real chance to reclaim the seat given the underlying ward history and the closeness of the by-election margin. Minor party probabilities are set low but nonzero to reflect irreducible uncertainty in a five-candidate FPTP race.

Moorland has trended Labour since 2022: Labour won in 2022 (701 v 644), 2023 (768 v 535), and 2024 (667 v 479), with Fido's Conservative vote share collapsing from 47.9% (his 2021 win) to 32.7% in 2024. This gives Labour's Adeyemi a clear incumbency-style advantage as the most recent three-time ward winner's party, though she personally has weaker vote-share history in other wards (13-19%), suggesting some candidate-quality uncertainty. Fido is a known local figure (ex-councillor, teacher, active community profile) which could partially offset the negative trend, but the multi-year swing away from the Conservatives is the dominant ward-level signal. The wildcard is Reform's Liam Kelly, who won a Lincolnshire County Council division within Lincoln in May 2025 with 40.7% of the vote, evidencing real Reform strength in the city that didn't exist in the 2021-2024 Moorland contests (Reform's best previous Moorland showing was just 10.6% in 2024). Given the national and local rightward fragmentation trend (Reform siphoning votes disproportionately from Conservatives), Kelly is a credible threat to overtake Fido and potentially challenge Labour, especially if he consolidates most of the declining Tory vote plus some anti-incumbent protest votes. However, translating a county-level personal/geographic base into this specific city ward carries uncertainty, and Labour's three-cycle winning streak with growing margins makes them the modal favorite. Green and Lib Dem candidates have consistently polled in single digits in this ward and are not competitive for the seat itself, though they add some vote-splitting texture. Overall this points to a three-way plausible contest dominated by Labour as favorite, Reform as a rising credible second, and the Conservative incumbent-challenger as a fading but not negligible third, with Green and Lib Dem effectively out of contention for the win.

Prettygate has been a reliably Conservative-held ward across all four contests from 2021-2024, with the Conservatives winning every time (including a razor-thin 22-vote margin in 2023, but a comfortable ~600-vote margin in 2024). This gives the Conservative line a strong structural advantage. However, several factors temper that advantage for 2026: (1) the Conservative candidate, David Linghorn-Baker, has no personal electoral track record of winning and his prior vote shares in other wards (33%, 16%, 17%) are markedly weaker than the sitting Conservative councillors (Lissimore, Tate, Buston) who built strong personal votes in Prettygate; the seat lacks an incumbency effect this cycle. (2) John Loxley (Lib Dem) has contested this exact seat four consecutive times, building sustained name recognition and finishing a clear second every time, peaking at 41.3% in 2023 (a near-win) and still polling 32% in 2024 even amid a strong Conservative rebound. His consistent local presence and status as the default anti-Conservative choice make him a credible challenger. (3) The entry of Reform UK (Sophie Preston-Hall, previously a modest performer at 6-12% in a different ward) introduces a real risk of splitting the Conservative-leaning vote, which could meaningfully erode the Conservative margin even if Reform itself is unlikely to win outright in a ward with no prior Reform base. (4) Labour and the Greens have consistently underperformed in Prettygate (13% and under 10% respectively) and have no signs of a breakthrough, keeping their win probabilities low. Given the ward's persistent (if narrowing at times) Conservative lean weighed against a weaker specific candidate, a strong perennial Lib Dem challenger, and vote-splitting risk from Reform, I treat this as a competitive two-horse race tilted slightly toward the Conservatives, with a modest but real chance for Reform to overperform its prior fringe-ward results, and minimal chances for Labour or the Greens.

This ward was solidly Labour from 2021-2024 (Labour winning each regular election, typically 45-55% of the vote against fragmented opposition), but the most recent and most directly relevant data point is the May 2025 by-election, in which Reform UK's Anthony Payne defeated Labour's candidate 789 to 630 votes (34.8% to 27.9%), a margin of about 7 points on similar turnout (34%) to prior regular elections. Payne is now the sitting councillor defending the seat, which typically provides some incumbency advantage, and Reform also posted a solid 26.8% in a nearby Devon county division in the same month, suggesting the by-election result reflected a genuine local swing rather than a one-off protest vote. Labour's replacement candidate, Steve Casemore, has a weaker electoral track record in other areas (2-6% vote shares), though this is not directly diagnostic of his prospects in this specific ward. There is no independent candidate this time (the 2025 independent took ~11% of the vote), and it is unclear whether those voters will consolidate behind Reform (plausible if protest-driven) or return to Labour, adding some uncertainty to the race. Given Labour's historical base and the possibility of reversion in a full-turnout, non-by-election context, Labour remains a credible second-place threat, but Reform enters as the modest favorite given the very recent, decisive local result and incumbency. The Conservative, Green, and Liberal Democrat candidates have consistently polled in the single digits to low teens in this ward over multiple recent elections and are not seen as realistic contenders for first place, though the Green candidate's ~11% floor gives it a marginally higher chance than the other minor parties.

Amanda Napper (Reform UK) is the sitting councillor, having won this exact ward's by-election just twelve months earlier (May 2025) with a commanding 58.26% of the vote, more than 25 points ahead of second-placed Labour (31.9%) and far ahead of the Conservatives (9.9%). This represents a dramatic swing from the ward's 2021-2024 pattern, when it was reliably won by Labour/Independent candidates and Reform UK polled only in the low single digits to low teens (e.g., 263 votes/roughly a quarter of the winner's tally in 2024). Such a large, recent swing toward Reform, combined with incumbency advantage from having just been elected, makes Napper the clear favourite to retain the seat in this scheduled May 2026 election, though by-election results can overstate true underlying support due to unusual turnout dynamics (turnout was low, 26%), so some reversion toward Labour is plausible, especially with a full slate of Labour campaign infrastructure evident (dedicated Facebook page for 'Throston Ward Labour'). Jack Callaghan (Labour) is the clearest challenger, given Labour's consistent second-place finishes and prior ward dominance 2021-2024. The Conservative candidate, Margaret Lyall, has a modest track record (15-18% in other wards) but no history in Throston and faces a squeezed position between Reform and Labour. The Liberal Democrat and Green candidates are first-time entrants in this ward with no local electoral record, typically achieving low single-digit shares in similar Hartlepool contests, so they are assigned small residual probabilities. Overall, Napper is favoured but not assured, reflecting uncertainty about whether the extraordinary by-election swing will fully persist in a normal-turnout scheduled election a year later.__

Killingbeck & Seacroft has been an extremely safe Labour ward across every election from 2021-2024, with Labour winning by margins of roughly 45-50 points each time (e.g., 66% vs 12% Conservative in 2024, 62% vs 20% Conservative in 2022). John Tudor, the Labour candidate for 2026, is the sitting councillor who already won this exact ward in 2022 with nearly 62% of the vote, giving him strong incumbency recognition and an established local base. None of the challengers show signs of being able to close a gap of this size: the Conservative vote has been steadily declining (from 1145 votes in 2021 to just 481 in 2024), the Green vote has hovered in the 300-400 range, the Liberal Democrat candidate has a weak track record even in other wards (1-2% vote shares), and the SDP candidate's historical results are negligible (under 1.2% each time). Reform UK, represented here by David Dresser (who previously stood for the Lib Dems and Reform in other wards with modest 6-10% results), is a live wildcard given national polling momentum for Reform in 2025-2026, and Reform's Leeds vote share has been growing (10.3% in a 2024 Leeds ward). However, even a strong Reform surge would need to overturn a 40+ point deficit relative to Labour's historical dominance here, which is a high bar for a single by-election-style local contest absent specific local scandal or collapse signals. Given the extremely stable historical baseline and lack of any indication of Labour vulnerability in the packet, Labour is assigned a dominant probability of winning, with Reform as the most plausible (though still unlikely) challenger, and Conservative, Green, Lib Dem, and SDP trailing further behind in win probability given their historical vote shares in this ward.

Bowthorpe was a safe Labour ward in every regular May election from 2021-2024, with Labour winning by margins of 500-900+ votes each time (e.g. 1201 vs 342 in 2024). However, the May 2025 by-election upended this pattern: Labour's Richard Lawes scraped home by just one vote (689-688) over Reform UK's Nick Taylor, with the Green candidate also surging to 430 votes, while the Conservative and Lib Dem candidates collapsed to their weakest-ever showings (281 and 102 respectively). Crucially, turnout in that by-election (2,190 combined top-vote total) was comparable to or higher than prior regular contests, suggesting this was a genuine electoral shift rather than a low-turnout fluke. For 2026, the ballot lineup mirrors the 2025 by-election almost exactly (same Reform candidate Nick Taylor, a new Green candidate, weak Lib Dem and Conservative options), implying the Labour-Reform battle is likely to be the decisive contest again. Labour has fielded Mike Sands, who previously won this very seat outright in 2022 (1170 votes), giving Labour a recognizable, previously successful candidate that may provide a modest edge over a fresh nominee. Given Reform's demonstrated ability to nearly match Labour turnout-for-turnout in the most recent contest and continued momentum for insurgent right-populist parties in England's 2026 local cycle, this is treated as a near-toss-up between Labour and Reform, with Labour receiving a slight edge due to incumbency-adjacent brand strength and the ward's longer Labour history. Green support has grown sharply (226 to 430 votes between 2024 and 2025) and could plausibly rise further, but starting from third place makes an outright win unlikely absent a major swing. Conservative and Liberal Democrat vote shares have been declining or stagnant for several cycles and neither party shows signs of the organizational surge needed to contend for first place in this ward.

This ward has been solidly Labour at the ballot-box level from 2021-2023 (Labour swept all three seats in 2021, and Labour candidates Ahmed and Ali won comfortably in 2022 and 2023). The only Labour loss came in 2024, when the sitting Labour councillor (Naeem Ashraf, elected 2021) defected to run as an Independent and won in a landslide (1789 vs 586) with no Labour candidate even contesting that seat — evidence that personal/community-based independent votes can dwarf party-label voting in this ward, a pattern common in Lancashire mill-town wards with strong biraderi/community networks. The seat up in 2026 corresponds to the rotation slot last contested in 2022, which Sajjad Ahmed won for Labour (1579 votes, 48.3%, beating the Conservative by a relatively narrow margin). Ahmed is now standing as an Independent in 2026, mirroring Ashraf's earlier trajectory, and brings incumbency, name recognition, and a proven personal vote in this exact seat. Unlike 2024, however, Labour has fielded a fresh candidate (Adam Walsh, no electoral record) to contest the seat directly against him, which should split the traditionally Labour-leaning vote rather than hand it entirely to the independent. Given the strong local precedent for defecting incumbents outperforming their old party, Ahmed is favored, but Walsh retains real chances given Labour's underlying brand strength in this ward (as shown by Ali's landslide as a loyal Labour candidate in 2023). The Conservative vote has been steadily eroding ward-over-ward (from ~45% in 2022 to under 25% in 2023-2024), so Pauline McCormick is a distant contender. Reform's John Metcalfe has no history in this specific ward but posted a strong 32% in a separate, likely more favorable (whiter, more rural) Lancashire County Council division in 2025, suggesting some latent regional strength but considerable uncertainty about transferability to this different, more urban/minority-community ward. Green and Liberal Democrat candidates have consistently polled in the low single digits here and in similar wards, so they are given minimal winning probability.

Sutton and Mount Gould has been an extremely safe Labour ward across every regular May election from 2021-2024, with Labour consistently taking 56-58% of the vote and winning by margins of 1,000+ votes over a fragmented field (Conservatives typically second at 12-25%, Greens rising from 7% to ~11-12%, Reform debuting at 8.4% in 2024, Lib Dems around 5-6%, TUSC 2-3%). The Labour candidate, Mary Aspinall, previously won this exact seat outright in 2022 with 57% of the vote, giving her strong incumbent-adjacent recognition and a proven local base. Baseline historical data alone would suggest Labour is an overwhelming favorite. However, the forecast date (May 2026) falls amid a well-documented national collapse in Labour's polling position and a surge for Reform UK and, in many urban/left-leaning wards, the Green Party — a dynamic explicitly invoked in the Green candidate's own statement, which claims 'Labour's vote share is collapsing' and that the contest for second is 'close between the Greens and Reform,' implying Labour's lead has narrowed enough that unseating them is plausible. This is self-serving campaign rhetoric and should be discounted, but it is consistent with broader documented volatility in England's 2025-2026 local political landscape and with the Green party's genuine upward trajectory in this ward across 2022-2024. Some caution is warranted about how seriously to take Reform as an imminent threat: the Reform candidate's own public statement is generic boilerplate referencing 'Devonport' rather than Sutton and Mount Gould, suggesting a less locally tailored campaign than the Green candidate's, which may undercut claims of a genuine Reform surge specifically in this ward despite the national narrative. Given Labour's historically enormous margin (40+ points), even a substantial anti-incumbent swing driven by national dissatisfaction would likely leave Labour still ahead, though the race is plausibly closer than in 2021-2024. I weight Labour as the clear favorite but meaningfully discount their probability from near-certainty to account for compounding recent electoral volatility, with the Green Party as the most credible challenger given their consistent local growth trend and explicit campaign narrative, followed by a lesser but non-trivial chance for Reform UK reflecting the general national mood, with Conservatives continuing their multi-cycle decline in this ward, and Liberal Democrats/TUSC remaining marginal based on their persistently low vote shares in this specific ward across three prior contests.

Fratton is a genuine multi-party marginal. Across the four most recent contested elections in this ward (2021-24), the Liberal Democrats won three times (2021, 2023, 2024) and Labour once (2022, when Tom Coles took the seat now up for re-election with 43.5%). Underlying ward vote shares show a clear trend: the Conservative vote has collapsed (24%→17%→9%→8%), while the independent/insurgent-right vote associated with Jacob Short has risen sharply as 'Portsmouth Independent Party' (16.6% in 2023, then 28.0% in 2024, narrowly missing second place ahead of Labour). Short is now standing under the Reform UK banner, a switch that plausibly carries forward and likely amplifies his personal momentum given Reform's broader national surge through 2024-25, while also removing some localist-only appeal - a wash overall but directionally still upward. Labour's Tom Coles benefits from incumbency (he has held this specific seat since 2022) and a solid personal-vote base, but mid-term local elections are historically difficult for the governing party nationally, which could erode his 2022 vote share. The Liberal Democrats field a new candidate (Ansell) but with strong local infrastructure, sitting ward colleagues' endorsement, and the best recent won-loss record in this ward (winning the other two ward seats in 2021, 2023 and 2024), suggesting resilient local machine strength even though their vote share has been gently eroding as Reform/independent support rises. The Green candidate is new to Fratton (prior results are from Copnor) and Fratton's Green vote has consistently been in the 4-8% range with no plausible path to first place. The Conservative candidate has a weak recent record (8% in 2024) and faces continued squeeze from Reform. Given the closely bunched historical vote shares among Labour, Lib Dem and the Reform-aligned candidate, and no seat-specific 2026 polling available, I model this as a near three-way toss-up with a slight edge to the Lib Dems for their superior recent ward-winning record and organizational depth, Reform a close second given its strong and rising trajectory, and Labour a competitive third weighed down by incumbency-vs-national-mood offsetting factors. Conservative and Green are given small residual probabilities reflecting their consistently weak and declining vote shares.

New Bilton has been a safe Labour ward from 2021-2024, with Labour winning by comfortable margins (roughly 250-480 votes) over a distant-second Conservative party, while Green and Lib Dem candidates consistently polled in single digits. However, the May 2025 by-election marked a sharp shift: Labour's Angela Thompson (the current incumbent and 2026 candidate) won narrowly (466 votes, 34.3%) over a surging Reform UK candidate (410 votes, 30.2%), with the Conservative candidate falling to a distant third (241 votes) and Green/Lib Dem shares also compressed. This indicates Reform has displaced the Conservatives as the main challenger to Labour in this ward, consistent with Reform's strong national momentum through 2025. Reinforcing this, Reform's 2026 candidate Anne-Marie Sonko won a nearby Warwickshire county division (Earl Craven) in May 2025 with 33.7% of the vote, demonstrating an ability to convert Reform's national surge into actual electoral wins locally, even though that specific post covers a different (likely larger) area than New Bilton ward. The Conservative candidate for 2026, Andrew Bearne, has no prior electoral record, suggesting a weaker local profile, consistent with the Conservatives' declining position in this ward. Green (Sandison) and Lib Dem (Moran) candidates have long, consistent track records of low single-to-low-double-digit vote shares in this and similar wards, making a win highly unlikely for either. Given the tightness of the 2025 by-election result, the clear two-horse dynamic between Labour and Reform, and the strong pro-Reform national environment continuing into 2026, I treat this as close to a toss-up between the Labour incumbent (retaining some incumbency and ground-game advantage) and the Reform challenger (riding strong momentum), with the other three candidates given only marginal chances reflecting historical performance and lack of signs of a breakthrough.

Eccles is a heavily entrenched Labour ward: Labour has won all four contests since 2021, with vote shares ranging from ~16% (multi-seat 2021 split across three Labour candidates) up to 60%+ in single-seat years, and most recently 63% of the vote in 2024 (2121/3363) against a fragmented opposition (Conservatives 565, Green 479, TUSC 198). The Labour candidate, Nathaniel Djangmah Tetteh, is the sitting councillor for this ward (elected in 2021 and 2022) with a personal incumbency advantage, a positive community-focused statement, and no signs of local scandal or organized anti-Labour tactical coordination in the packet. The main opposition parties have consistently underperformed: Conservatives have declined from ~800 votes in 2021 to ~565 in 2024 as their vote share erodes nationally in many urban wards; Greens have modestly grown (from low hundreds to ~480 votes, roughly 14% in 2024) but remain a distant second; Liberal Democrats and TUSC poll in low single digits with no organizational presence suggesting a breakthrough. Reform UK is a new entrant with no prior ward-level record, but given their strong national polling surge through 2025-2026 and their tendency to attract protest votes in areas with previous Conservative or disengaged voters, they are treated as the most likely challenger to eat into the non-Labour vote, though starting from zero local infrastructure in a ward Labour has dominated for decades makes an outright win unlikely in a single cycle. Overall, Labour's structural advantage (fragmented opposition needing to unite over 60% of the vote against them) makes the incumbent the strong favorite, while acknowledging non-trivial uncertainty from national volatility (Reform surge, cost-of-living discontent) that could compress margins more than historical patterns suggest.'

Crookes & Crosspool has been a genuine Labour/Lib Dem marginal across 2021-2024, with Labour winning three of the four contests (2021, 2022, 2024) and the Lib Dems winning narrowly in 2023. The seat up in 2026 was last contested in 2022, when incumbent Minesh Parekh (Lab Co-op) won with 37.46% against Jordan Barry-style Lib Dem opposition at 34.53% - a fairly tight 3-point margin. Since then, ward-wide Labour performance strengthened (48.3% vs 31.9% in the 2024 contest for a different seat), suggesting some positive momentum for Labour locally, but the Lib Dems have consistently been the clear second force in every single election in this ward (never below ~32% when their strongest candidate stood) and are fielding Jordan Barry again, who personally secured 31.93% here in 2024 and has now run in this ward twice. This makes Parekh the favourite but leaves a live, competitive threat from Barry, especially given typical mid-term drag on a governing party (Labour has been the UK government since 2024) that is not directly evidenced in the packet but is a reasonable prior for this cycle. The Green candidate, Dylan Lewis-Creser, has shown rising personal vote shares elsewhere (11% to 32% between 2023 and 2024 in different wards), but the ward's own Green ceiling has been 13-19% historically, well behind the top two, so a win is unlikely though not negligible given the candidate's apparent campaigning strength. Reform UK (Ricia Ward) has no local track record but reflects a nationally rising minor-party trend that could produce a meaningful but sub-winning vote share in a ward that is relatively affluent/suburban and not historically fertile for insurgent right-wing parties. The Conservative candidate has a weak personal record (3.46% in a different ward) and Conservatives have never exceeded single digits here, while TUSC has always been a marginal fringe candidate. Overall, this remains fundamentally a two-horse race between Labour and the Liberal Democrats, with Labour slightly favoured due to incumbency and recent ward momentum, tempered by a credible Lib Dem challenger and some uncertainty from national political conditions not captured in the packet.

Holywell has been a safe-looking Labour ward in every regular May election from 2021-2024, with Labour winning by large margins (roughly 800-900 vote leads over second place). However, the most recent contest -- the May 2025 by-election, held on the same day as other 2025 local elections and with a comparable total turnout (~1,730 votes) to prior regular elections -- was extremely close: incumbent Labour candidate Asma Saeed Suleman won with only a 41-vote margin (573 to 532) over the Liberal Democrat candidate, a dramatic tightening from the ~300-450 vote gaps typical of 2021-2024. This suggests a genuine shift in local political sentiment (consistent with broader 2025 UK local trends of Labour vote softening and Liberal Democrat/Reform gains) rather than a low-turnout anomaly. For 2026, Suleman runs as the sitting councillor with only ~10 months of incumbency in a seat she barely held, facing a new Liberal Democrat candidate (Emil Rowe) in a ward where the Lib Dem vote nearly overtook Labour, and a Reform UK candidate (Michael Barrett) entering for the first time in this ward after a strong 17% showing in a nearby Watford by-election (Oxhey) reflecting Reform's continuing national momentum through 2025-2026. The Conservative vote share has been on a clear multi-year decline (24%->19%->9%->10%) and is unlikely to recover to competitive levels, while the Green (Jake Mitchell, ~7% last time) and TUSC (Mark O'Connor, perennial ~1-3%) candidates remain minor players unlikely to win but capable of drawing marginal votes that could affect the top-two race. Given Labour's underlying incumbency and historical structural advantage in this ward, but the credible and recent evidence of a near-toppling by the Liberal Democrats plus a new Reform threat splitting the right-of-Labour vote in unpredictable ways, I treat this as a genuinely competitive two-and-a-half horse race: Labour retains a modest edge due to incumbency and longer-run dominance, Liberal Democrats are a strong second contender given their near-win last year, and Reform UK has meaningful but less certain upside given no prior ward-specific data. Conservative, Green, and TUSC are given small residual probabilities reflecting their consistent minor-party status and declining or stagnant vote shares.

Peartree has been a rock-solid Liberal Democrat ward since 2021: LD candidates won the 2021 all-out election, the 2022, 2023 and 2024 annual elections, and the May 2025 by-election, each time comfortably beating Labour into second place. Duncan Merlin Jones is not just the LD standard-bearer but the incumbent for this specific seat class, having won it outright in 2022 with 51.8% of the vote (770 votes) and having served since as a Cabinet member (Resources), giving him strong name recognition and a personal vote on top of the party brand. His statement stresses continuity of local casework and a distinctive local issue (a proposed Welwyn Garden City Town Council), typical of an effective incumbent campaign. That said, the most recent contest (the May 2025 by-election) showed real erosion of the traditional LD dominance: Reform UK's Mark Biddle surged to second place with 23.1% (375 votes) against the LD's 34.2% (420 votes), a far closer race than in 2021-2024, while Labour and the Conservatives both collapsed to single-digit/high-teens shares. This suggests Reform UK, now fielding Ian Colpitts, could plausibly build on that by-election showing and mount a genuine challenge, especially if turnout patterns and national tailwinds favour Reform in 2026. However, by-elections often overstate insurgent-party performance relative to full-cycle elections with a well dug-in incumbent, and there is no evidence Reform has since built local organisational depth (no prior electoral record for Colpitts). Labour's own trajectory in the ward has been weak and worsening (down from ~26-28% in 2021-2024 to just 15.4% in the 2025 by-election), and its candidate's recent county council result (13.5% vote share) suggests continuing local weakness.
