Still, the ward history and Conservative local organisation point to a Conservative hold more often than not. Reform has some prior Colchester candidacy record at 6–12% elsewhere but no demonstrated Prettygate base, so a win would require a much larger breakthrough than evidenced in the packet. Labour and Green have been consistently well behind in Prettygate, with Labour around 12–13% and Green around 4–6%, leaving them only remote upset paths.

Reform UK is the favourite because the same candidate, Anthony John Payne, won the most recent same-ward contest in 2025 with 789 votes and 34.83%, ahead of Labour on 630 votes. That result was at normal recent turnout for the ward and is highly relevant: it is the newest same-post evidence, and Payne is standing again. Labour remains a serious challenger rather than an outsider because Mincinglake & Whipton was strongly Labour in 2021, 2022 and 2023, Labour still finished second in the 2025 by-election, and the 2026 race has no Independent candidate, so some 2024/2025 non-major-party vote could return to Labour. However, Labour's 2025 deficit, the absence of an incumbent Labour candidate in the packet, and the Reform candidate's demonstrated ward win make Reform more likely than Labour. The Conservative, Green and Liberal Democrat candidates have much lower chances: their parties have been consistently well behind the top two in recent same-ward elections, although the Green candidate has some same-ward/county-division presence and the Conservatives retain a small residual possibility if the Reform vote recedes sharply. The Liberal Democrats have the weakest recent ward record.

The strongest evidence is the most recent same-ward contest: in the 2025 Throston by-election Amanda Elizabeth Napper won for Reform UK with 889 votes, 58.3%, well ahead of Labour on 486 and the Conservatives on 151, and she is standing again. That gives Reform a clear lead and a candidate/local-recognition advantage. However, I do not treat it as safe: Labour won Throston in 2022, 2023, and 2024, including a large 2024 win, so the ward has a real Labour baseline and the 2024-to-2025 swing shows local results can be volatile. Labour is therefore the only substantial challenger. The Conservatives have some prior presence but were far behind in 2025 and would need both Reform and Labour to underperform sharply. The Liberal Democrats and Greens have no ward or candidate electoral record in the packet, so their win paths are very small.

Labour is an overwhelming favourite on the supplied evidence. In Killingbeck & Seacroft, Labour won every listed regular May election from 2021 to 2024 with very stable vote shares around 61–65%: 2946 votes in 2021, 2510 in 2022, 2446 in 2023, and 2551 in 2024. The opposition has been fragmented and far behind: the 2024 runner-up Conservative vote was 481, Reform was 435, Green was 407, Liberal Democrat was 183, and SDP was 106, while Labour had 2551. Even the combined non-Labour vote in 2024 was well short of Labour. The Labour candidate, John Tudor, also has a prior same-ward win in 2022 with 61.93%. The main uncertainty is ordinary low-turnout local-election volatility and the possibility of a large political realignment or concentrated anti-Labour vote, with Reform, Conservative, or Green each having some same-ward or nearby evidence of a modest base. But based only on the packet, any non-Labour candidate would need an exceptionally large swing from the historical pattern, so their individual win probabilities remain low.

Labour is the favorite, but not a safe one. Bowthorpe was very strongly Labour in the regular ward elections from 2021 through 2024, with Labour vote totals around 1,170-1,200 and large leads. The 2026 Labour candidate, Mike Sands, also appears in the ward history as the 2022 Labour winner, which supports a Labour incumbency/local-recognition advantage. However, the most recent same-ward contest, the 2025 by-election, was essentially tied: Labour won by only one vote over Nick Taylor of Reform UK, who is standing again in 2026. That makes Reform a clearly credible challenger rather than a minor-party outsider. The 2025 result may have had some by-election/protest character and Labour may recover in a regular scheduled contest with a previously successful candidate, so I keep Labour ahead, but the one-vote 2025 margin prevents high confidence. The Greens were a solid but still clearly third-place force in 2025 and have some outside chance if Labour and Reform split the vote and Green support rises further. The Conservatives and Liberal Democrats have prior Bowthorpe presence but were well behind in 2025 and have little evident path to first place, especially with Reform contesting the right-of-centre vote.

This ward has a strong recent pattern of Labour or Labour-derived local candidates winning: Labour won all three seats in 2021, Sajjad Ahmed won the same ward for Labour in 2022, Labour won comfortably in 2023, and an Independent previously elected for Labour won overwhelmingly in 2024 when Labour did not stand. That makes the main contest likely to be between Sajjad Ahmed as an Independent incumbent/former Labour winner and the official Labour candidate, Adam Thomas Walsh. Ahmed gets a slight edge because he has the strongest same-post personal record and is plausibly the incumbent defending the seat, but the Labour label has repeatedly been strong here and Walsh could consolidate voters who prefer the party over the former Labour independent. Reform is a real spoiler and possible winner: John Metcalfe polled 32.38% in a 2025 Lancashire contest, and a Labour/Independent split could allow a plurality win in the high 20s or low 30s. However, Reform has no same-ward history in the packet and also faces competition from the Conservative candidate. The Conservatives have historic second-place strength in this ward, including a very close 2022 result, but their more recent same-ward totals were much lower and Reform is likely to divide the non-Labour/non-independent vote, so they are a secondary possibility. Liberal Democrats and Greens have only low prior local vote shares and no evidence of being competitive for first place.

Labour is the clear favourite on the supplied evidence. Sutton and Mount Gould has been strongly and consistently Labour in the same-post regular elections: Labour won 51.5% in 2021, 57.1% in 2022, 58.6% in 2023, and 56.4% in 2024, with a stable Labour vote around 1,700-1,785 on roughly 3,000 total votes. Mary Elizabeth Aspinall is also the sitting Labour and Co-operative councillor elected for this ward in 2022, which adds candidate-specific strength. The nearest non-Labour vote in 2024 was only 449 Conservative votes, while Green had 345 and Reform 252, so defeating Labour would require both a very large Labour collapse and substantial consolidation behind one challenger despite a split field. I nevertheless leave a meaningful upset probability because the 2026 environment is uncertain from the packet and the Green statement claims Labour is falling while Greens and Reform are surging. The Green candidate has the strongest local campaign signal among the challengers and the party has an established ward base around 11-13% in recent years, so Greens are the leading upset route. Reform also has a plausible route if it absorbs much of the declining Conservative vote and benefits from broader anti-Labour protest, but its candidate statement/profile appear less tailored to this ward. Conservatives have historical second-place strength but have declined sharply and now face Reform competition. Liberal Democrats and TUSC have recent same-ward records in the mid-single digits and low single digits respectively, making their win paths very remote.

This is a genuinely competitive three-way ward on the supplied evidence. The Liberal Democrats have the strongest recent party record in Fratton, winning in 2021, 2023 and 2024, including the most recent contest, and their 2026 candidate has a locally focused statement tying him to existing Lib Dem councillors and ward work. However, the 2026 Labour candidate, Tom Coles, is the same candidate who won the 2022 Fratton contest with 43.5% and is very likely the councillor defending that seat, giving Labour a meaningful incumbency/personal-vote advantage over the weaker Labour showings in 2023 and 2024. Jacob Lee Short is also a serious contender: he rose from 16.6% in 2023 to 28.0% in 2024 as Portsmouth Independent Party, finishing only about 95 votes behind the winning Lib Dem and effectively tied with Labour, and now stands as Reform UK. That label could help consolidate some anti-major-party or ex-Conservative support, but it also creates uncertainty because his supplied voter statement still emphasizes Portsmouth Independents and independence from national parties. The Conservatives and Greens have recent Fratton performances around the high single digits or below and would need an implausibly large jump to win. Overall I rate Labour narrowly ahead because of Tom Coles's prior same-ward win and likely incumbency, with the Lib Dems close behind due to repeated ward wins, and Reform close enough to win if Short retains his 2024 personal vote and adds some Conservative/right-of-centre support.

Labour is the favourite because New Bilton has elected Labour in every listed contest from 2021 through the 2025 by-election, and Angela Mary Thompson is the same Labour candidate who won the 2025 same-ward by-election. However, the 2025 result was close: Labour 466 versus Reform 410, with the Conservatives well back on 241. That makes the seat genuinely competitive rather than a safe Labour hold. Reform is the main challenger: the party was only about four percentage points behind in the ward in 2025, and Anne-Marie Sonko has a strong supplied record as an elected Reform county councillor in 2025 with 33.71% elsewhere in Warwickshire. I still give Labour an edge from repeated ward-level wins and same-ward incumbency, but with substantial uncertainty. The Conservative candidate has no supplied personal electoral record and the party fell to third once Reform stood in 2025, so a Conservative win would likely require major Reform underperformance or vote consolidation not evidenced in the packet. The Green and Liberal Democrat candidates have records and/or ward histories in the single digits to low teens, leaving them only remote upset chances.

Labour is a very strong favourite on the supplied evidence. Eccles has returned Labour comfortably in every listed recent election: in the comparable single-seat contests Labour won with 1,964 votes in 2022, 2,022 in 2023, and 2,121 in 2024, while the nearest opposition candidate had 731, 482, and 565 votes respectively. Nathaniel Djangmah Tetteh is also a sitting/previously elected Labour councillor for the ward and has a current voter statement, which reinforces the incumbency advantage. The opposition vote is fragmented among Reform UK, Conservative, Green, Liberal Democrat, and TUSC candidates. Reform UK is given the largest non-Labour chance because it is a new entrant with uncertain upside and could plausibly become the main anti-Labour vehicle, but there is no local track record in the packet showing it can approach Labour's vote. The Greens have shown some growth locally and have a local statement, while the Conservatives have historically been the main runner-up but now face possible competition from Reform. The Liberal Democrats and TUSC have low recent local bases, with TUSC repeatedly polling only a small share despite candidate continuity. Overall, Labour would need an unusually large adverse swing or opposition consolidation to lose, so its win probability is high but not absolute.

Labour is the clear but not certain favourite. Crookes & Crosspool has been principally a Labour–Liberal Democrat marginal: Labour won in 2021 by about 4 points, Labour’s Minesh Parekh won in 2022 by about 3 points, the Liberal Democrats won in 2023 by about 3 points, and Labour won in 2024 by a much larger margin of about 16 points. The 2026 Labour candidate is Parekh, a sitting Labour & Co-operative councillor with a prior same-ward win, which is a material advantage. The Liberal Democrat candidate, Jordan Barry, also has recent same-ward experience and took 31.93% in 2024, so the LDs remain a serious challenger, especially given their 2023 ward win and the ward’s history of close Labour–LD contests. However, the most recent ward result was strongly Labour and Barry was the defeated LD candidate in that contest, so Labour retains the edge. The Green Party has a credible candidate with past electoral activity and the party has polled in the mid-teens to high-teens here, but it has not been close to winning this ward in the supplied history; a win would require a large realignment or a severe Labour/LD split. Conservatives, TUSC, and Reform UK have no demonstrated path from the supplied ward history: Conservative support has been low and Reform is new to the ward record, though it could take some protest/right-leaning votes. Overall this is forecast as Labour-leaning with substantial LD upset risk and a small Green outside chance.

Labour is the favourite, but the seat is not treated as safe because the most recent Holywell contest was close. In the regular ward elections from 2021 to 2024, Labour won every time with large leads and vote shares above 50%. However, the 2025 Holywell by-election, with a six-party field similar to 2026, was much tighter: Labour won 573 votes to the Liberal Democrats' 532, with Reform on 294, Conservatives on 180, Greens on 126 and TUSC on 23. I weight that 2025 result heavily because it is the most recent same-ward evidence and the Labour candidate is the same person who won it, but I also shrink toward the earlier pattern of repeated Labour strength in the ward. The Liberal Democrats are therefore the main alternative winner: they were only 41 votes behind in 2025, though their 2026 candidate has no prior record in the packet and the party's pre-2025 Holywell vote was much lower. Reform has a meaningful but distant chance given its 17% Holywell showing in 2025 and this candidate's similar prior result in Oxhey, but would need a substantial further advance and a split at the top. The Conservative candidate has past Holywell experience but the party fell to about 10% in the 2025 contest with Reform present, making a win unlikely. Green and TUSC have no demonstrated path to first place from their recorded Holywell vote levels.

The Liberal Democrats are clear favourites: Peartree has elected a Liberal Democrat at every listed contest from 2021 through the 2025 by-election, and Duncan Merlin Jones is a known ward candidate who won this same ward in 2022 with 770 votes and 51.78%. The party also retained the ward in 2023, 2024, and the 2025 by-election. However, the 2025 by-election substantially lowers certainty: Reform UK, absent from earlier Peartree contests, came a close second with 375 votes to the Liberal Democrats' 420, while Labour fell from its 2021-2024 regular-election range of roughly 29-36% to about 20%. That makes Reform the main live challenger if its 2025 support persists or grows and if the Liberal Democrat vote remains closer to the by-election level than to Duncan Jones's 2022 performance. Labour has a plausible but smaller path because it was the regular main challenger before 2025, including 521 votes in 2024 versus 621 for the Liberal Democrats, but the 2025 collapse and a Reform presence make a Labour win less likely. The Conservatives have been weak since 2022 and are further squeezed by Reform; the Greens have a repeat local candidate and a statement but only around 7-8% in recent Peartree contests, leaving them with only a remote upset chance.

The Green candidate, Malcolm Wallace, is the clear favourite on the supplied evidence. Central Meon Valley has moved from a Conservative win in 2021 to three consecutive Green wins: Green shares of 55.5% in 2022, 63.1% in 2023, and 51.5% in 2024, with large margins over the Conservatives each time. Wallace himself won this ward in 2022 with 1,955 votes and 55.5%, and his profile also shows subsequent successful local campaigning and election to Hampshire County Council in the Meon Valley by-election in 2024. The Conservatives remain the main alternative because they won here in 2021 and have consistently finished second since, and David Newberry has prior local Conservative electoral experience, but the recent ward record gives them a substantial gap to close, made harder by the presence of Reform. The Liberal Democrat candidate has a credible local-government profile, and the party improved to 17.4% in the ward in 2024, but it has not recently been close to winning this ward. Reform has no prior local record in the packet but could draw a non-trivial protest/right-leaning vote; still, winning outright from no recorded base in a ward with a strong Green incumbent/local machine looks unlikely. Labour has been consistently very low in the ward history, so its win probability is only residual.

Labour is the narrow favorite because it won Buckingham in 2024 by a substantial margin, although its current candidate is new and the 2024 result may not fully carry over. The Conservative candidate has previously won this ward and remains the main challenger, with meaningful probability of recovering from the 2024 defeat. The Green candidate has demonstrated local electoral strength elsewhere in Adur, making a stronger showing plausible, but Green support in Buckingham was modest in the most recent ward election. Reform UK could draw support, particularly from the Conservative side, but the candidate has no supplied electoral record. The Liberal Democrat candidate has very limited prior support in the packet, so is assigned only a small winning probability.

Labour has won this ward in each of the last four contests recorded, including a narrow win in 2024, and its 2026 candidate has a relatively strong 2024 local-election result elsewhere in the borough. Abigail Compton-Burnett is the main alternative: she nearly won the ward in 2024 as a Conservative and had previously been elected here, but is now standing as an Independent, creating uncertainty about how much of her former Conservative support she retains. Mike Patchett has prior experience in the ward but was behind the leading Conservative candidates in 2021, and the Conservative vote may be divided between him and Compton-Burnett. Reform, Green and Liberal Democrat candidates have some evidence of local support but appear unlikely to top the poll absent a substantial change in the established Labour-Conservative pattern.

Labour is given the highest probability because Richard Swift previously won this ward for Labour in 2022, and Labour led the Liberal Democrats in the regular 2023 and 2024 ward contests. However, the latest contest was the 2025 by-election, where the Liberal Democrats won with 38.6% against Labour's 33.0%, so the Liberal Democrat candidate remains a strong contender despite having no recorded prior electoral results and not being the previous by-election winner. The Green candidate has a meaningful chance after taking 17.1% in that by-election and having a prior record as a councillor, but would need substantial further growth to win. Conservative, Reform, and Independent victory probabilities are low based on the available ward results and candidate histories.

The Conservative candidate is the clear favorite. Conservatives won every contest in the ward from 2021 through 2025, with roughly 44%–47% in the four most recent elections, and Eddie Fraser Reeves previously won this ward with 45.9%. Labour is the strongest established challenger: Annette Murphy rose from 22.4% to 33.2% between 2022 and 2024, and her return creates a plausible route to regain support after Labour fell to 14.5% with a different candidate in 2025. Reform UK has meaningful upset potential because it is new to the ward and could attract enough support to divide the right-of-centre vote, but there is no prior candidate or ward result establishing it as the leading challenger. The Liberal Democrat candidate has a substantial historical base but declined from 20.8% to 12.6% in the 2022–2024 contests, while the Green candidate has been consistently near 9%–11% and has little evidence of a winning coalition.

Martin Andrew Topp has the strongest position after winning the 2025 Chorley East election with 40.1% against Ian Daniel Cardwell's 36.93%, and he is standing again. That result makes Reform UK the modest favorite, with some additional support from incumbency and recent Reform performances elsewhere in Chorley. Labour remains a substantial threat: it had won every preceding ward contest in the packet, Cardwell was narrowly beaten in 2025, and a regular election could improve Labour turnout and organization. Mark Grandi has a history of attracting a meaningful Conservative minority but has not been close to winning; the Green and TUSC candidates have very limited demonstrated support, so their chances of topping the poll are remote.

Reform UK is given a narrow edge because Liam Mark Kelly won his 2025 Lincolnshire County Council division with 40.71%, indicating substantial recent local momentum, although Reform won only 10.3% in Moorland in 2024 and the county division is not the same contest. Labour retains a strong structural position after winning Moorland in 2022, 2023, and 2024, but Funmi Adeyemi has a relatively weak prior electoral record and is new to this ward. Matthew Fido has meaningful personal strength, having won Moorland in 2021 and taken 32.7% when losing in 2024, making the Conservatives a substantial third possibility. Green and Liberal Democrat wins are unlikely given their consistently low ward vote shares and the absence of evidence of a breakthrough. The contest is highly uncertain because the packet contains no 2026 polling or same-ward 2025 result.

This is primarily a two-way contest between the Liberal Democrats and Conservatives. The Conservatives have won every recent Prettygate election and led John Loxley by 606 votes in 2024, so their party base remains substantial. However, Loxley has repeatedly been the strongest non-Conservative candidate, nearly winning in 2023, and the Conservatives are replacing the recent ward winner with David Linghorn-Baker. Linghorn-Baker has meaningful local involvement and prior campaigning in Prettygate, but his personal electoral record elsewhere is not especially strong. Reform UK has demonstrated a potentially material vote in nearby Colchester elections and could draw enough support from the Conservatives to affect the result, while Labour and the Greens appear unlikely to win based on the supplied record.

Anthony John Payne and Reform UK are the clear forecast leaders: Payne won this ward's 2025 single-seat election with 34.83% and 789 votes, ahead of Labour on 27.79%, and is the only leading candidate returning from that contest. Labour remains the principal alternative given its wins in every ward contest from 2021 through 2024, but its 2026 candidate is different from the 2025 runner-up. The Conservative candidate has a stronger prior result elsewhere than the party achieved in this ward in 2025, but the ward history shows a substantial decline in Conservative support. The Green and Liberal Democrat candidates have comparatively weak ward records, making a win unlikely.

Amanda Elizabeth Napper is the clear favorite. She won the same Throston ward in the 2025 by-election with 889 votes and 58.26% of the vote, comfortably ahead of Labour on 486 and the Conservatives on 151, and she is now the incumbent seeking re-election. Labour has the strongest plausible route to victory given its long history of winning this ward and its second-place finish in 2025, so a substantial probability is retained for a Labour recovery. However, Labour would need a sizeable swing against an incumbent who recently demonstrated strong support. The Conservatives have some prior support in nearby Hartlepool contests but were a distant third in the latest Throston election, while the Liberal Democrat and Green candidates have no supplied prior electoral record and are unlikely to be leading contenders.

Labour is the clear favorite based on the supplied ward history: Labour won in every year from 2021 through 2024, with vote shares around 62–65%, and John Tudor personally won the ward in 2022 with 61.93%. The large and persistent historical margin supports a high probability of retention. Reform receives the largest non-Labour probability because it polled 10.7% in the ward in 2024 and its candidate has prior Reform experience, including 10.3% in another Leeds ward. The Conservative candidate has local ties and won 11.55% in the ward in 2024, but right-of-centre vote splitting and the party's declining ward vote reduce the chance of victory. Green has established third-party support in the ward but no prior record for this candidate, while the Liberal Democrat and Social Democratic Party candidates have historically attracted very small shares.

Labour is the slight favorite because it won every regular Bowthorpe election in the packet from 2021 through 2024, and Mike Sands is also the named Labour winner in 2022. However, that historical advantage weakened sharply in the 2025 by-election: Labour won by just one vote while Reform UK’s Nick Taylor nearly prevailed, making this a highly competitive Labour–Reform contest. Reform receives substantial probability because Taylor is returning after that near-win and had previously risen to 31.42% in a Norwich ward contest. The Green candidate has a meaningful outside chance given the party’s 2025 Bowthorpe vote and Amber Smith’s longstanding local connection and detailed community profile. The Conservatives and Liberal Democrats have weaker recent results in this ward, so their winning probabilities are very low.

The independent candidate, Sajjad Ahmed, has the strongest direct evidence: he won this exact ward in 2022 with 48.27% of the vote while standing for Labour, suggesting substantial ward familiarity and a possible personal vote despite changing labels. Labour remains highly competitive based on repeated wins in the ward, but its new candidate has no recorded electoral history and may face a split with Ahmed. Reform receives a substantial probability because its candidate won 32.38% in a nearby Pendle county division in 2025, indicating a potentially strong contemporary vote, though there is no same-ward result. The Conservatives were historically the main challenger but have declined sharply in this ward since 2022 and receive a lower probability. The Liberal Democrat and Green candidates have limited prior support and are assigned only small upset chances.

Labour and Co-operative candidate Mary Aspinall starts with the strongest structural position: she won this ward in 2022 with 57.07%, and Labour won the ward comfortably in every election from 2021 through 2024. Her incumbent status therefore merits the highest individual probability. However, the packet also indicates a potentially major 2026 realignment: the Green candidate describes Labour's support as collapsing and identifies a close Green–Reform contest, while Reform already reached 14.99% in a Plymouth ward in 2024 and may be benefiting from a broader surge. I therefore assign substantial probabilities to both the Green and Reform candidates, with the Green slightly ahead because the party has been the strongest established challenger in this ward and its candidate reports a strong local connection and active campaigning. Conservative prospects are low despite the party's former second-place history, given the absence of a candidate record and the apparent competition from Reform. The Liberal Democrat and TUSC candidates have weak prior results in this ward and receive only minimal probabilities.

The Liberal Democrats have the strongest recent ward record, winning Fratton in 2021, 2023 and 2024, and Billy Ansell presents as a locally rooted candidate supported by sitting Lib Dem councillors. Reform UK candidate Jacob Lee Short is also a serious contender because he finished second in 2024 as a local independent with 28.04% and may retain much of that personal vote, though the change of party label creates uncertainty. Labour remains competitive: Tom Coles won the ward in 2022 with 43.48%, but Labour finished third and nearly tied the independent candidate in 2024. The Conservative and Green candidates have little recent evidence of a winning coalition in this ward, so their win probabilities are very low.

Labour is the favorite because it has won every contest in the ward from 2021 through 2025, and Angela Mary Thompson is the sitting councillor. However, the 2025 result was much closer than earlier Labour victories: Labour won with 466 votes against Reform's 410, while the Conservative candidate received 241. Reform therefore has a substantial upset chance, particularly given Anne-Marie Sonko's election for Reform in Warwickshire in 2025. The Conservatives remain a plausible but clearly less likely challenger given their third-place showing in 2025 and the lack of a prior record for their current candidate. Green and Liberal Democrat victories are remote because their recent ward results have been well behind the two leading parties.

Nathaniel Djangmah Tetteh is the clear favorite. He is the incumbent Labour councillor, won the 2022 Eccles election with 60.17% of the vote, and Labour won the comparable 2021 and 2024 Eccles contests by very large margins. The main uncertainty is whether a broader shift away from Labour, potentially benefiting the new Reform UK candidate or the Conservatives, could overcome that established lead. The Green candidate has some local history and a meaningful prior vote share, but remains a substantial outsider; the Liberal Democrat and TUSC candidates have no evidence in the packet of a plausible winning base.

Labour is the favorite based on the ward's recent results and Minesh Parekh's prior win in Crookes & Crosspool. Labour won in 2022 with 2,492 votes and again in 2024 with 3,310, although the Liberal Democrats won the ward in 2023 and have remained the principal challenger; Jordan Barry received 2,188 votes here in 2024. The Liberal Democrat win probability therefore remains substantial. The Green Party has been competitive in the ward, but its recent Crookes & Crosspool totals were well behind the top two; Dylan Lewis-Creser's strong 2024 result in nearby Walkley raises the Green possibility but does not make them the leading contender. Conservative, Reform UK, and TUSC have little demonstrated winning strength in the supplied ward history, so their probabilities are very low.

Labour is the leading candidate because it has won every listed Holywell contest, and Asma Saeed Suleman is the sitting councillor after winning the 2025 contest. However, the 2025 result was very close: Labour led the Liberal Democrats by only 41 votes, so a substantial chance of a Liberal Democrat upset is warranted, especially given the party's repeated second-place finishes. Reform UK has a plausible but distinctly lower chance based on its strong 2025 showing, while the Conservative, Green and TUSC candidates have weak prospects in the supplied ward history.

The Liberal Democrat candidate is the strongest favorite. Liberal Democrats won every recorded Peartree contest from 2021 through 2025, Duncan Merlin Jones personally won the 2022 contest with 51.78%, and the party retains a clear historical base. However, the 2025 by-election was much more competitive: Reform UK finished only 45 votes behind the Liberal Democrats, making Reform the principal upset threat and assigning it a substantial winning probability. Labour has generally been the main historical challenger, but its recent Peartree result was well behind both Liberal Democrats and Reform, and its current candidate's supplied prior result was relatively modest in a neighboring ward. The Green candidate has a consistent but small vote base and the Conservative candidate has no supplied prior record in the ward, so both have very low chances of winning.

Malcolm Wallace is the clear favorite. The Green Party has won this ward in each of the last three elections, with substantial margins in 2022, 2023, and 2024, and Wallace has both strong prior results and local councillor experience. The Conservatives are the main challenger because they won the ward in 2021 and David Newberry has a strong prior local result, but their recent ward results have been well behind the Greens. The Liberal Democrats have a credible locally experienced candidate and were third with a meaningful share in 2024, while Reform UK and Labour have little supporting electoral evidence in this ward. The main uncertainty is the possibility of a Conservative recovery or a stronger-than-expected Liberal Democrat performance, so the Green probability is high but not near-certain.

Labour's David Joseph Devoy is the favorite because Labour won Buckingham decisively in the latest supplied ward election, taking 681 votes (50.9%) in 2024 against Kevin Boram's 424 (31.7%). Labour had also narrowed the Conservative lead to 78 votes in 2022, indicating a substantial ward-level shift before its 2024 victory. Boram remains the strongest challenger: he won this ward in 2021, has substantial local electoral experience, and could benefit from personal recognition, but his vote share fell from 49.7% in 2021 to 31.7% in 2024. Reform's entry creates additional uncertainty and may particularly complicate the Conservative route, although no local record is supplied for its candidate. Green candidate Gabrielle Crisp has previously won an Adur council seat and presents a locally focused profile, giving her more upset potential than the Green Party's weak 2024 Buckingham result alone would imply. The Liberal Democrats have some historical ward support but their candidate's only supplied result was 3.1% elsewhere in Adur. Uncertainty remains substantial because the latest same-ward result is two years old, several candidates lack electoral records, and a five-way FPTP contest can produce vote splitting.

Labour is the clear favorite because the ward's recent elections were consistently Labour-Conservative contests: Labour won in 2022 and 2023 and prevailed by 16 votes in 2024. In 2026, the opposition to Labour is unusually fragmented. Abigail Compton-Burnett, the 2024 Conservative runner-up and a former ward councillor, is now standing as an Independent against Conservative Mike Patchett, while Reform UK also provides another competing option. That division should make it substantially easier for Labour's Cait Fuller to win with a plurality. Compton-Burnett is the strongest challenger because of her recent 690-vote performance and established ward record, but it is uncertain how much of that support was personal rather than attached to her former Conservative label. Patchett also has local history, including 766 votes in the multi-member 2021 election, but must compete with both Compton-Burnett and Reform for voters who previously supported Conservatives. Reform's candidate recorded 7.99% in another ward in 2024, making a win possible only under a large and uncertain surge. The Liberal Democrats and Greens have remained in single digits in this ward or nearby contests and have very small winning chances.

Labour and the Liberal Democrats are the clear principal contenders. Labour won every regular ward contest in the packet from 2021 through 2024, and Richard Swift is a proven candidate who was elected here in 2022 with 1,390 votes. The Liberal Democrats, however, won the May 2025 by-election by 1,204 votes to Labour's 1,030, demonstrating that the ward is now highly competitive. Labour is rated a modest favorite because Swift brings prior ward-level success and likely incumbency, while Liberal Democrat Guy Mills has no listed electoral record and replaces the established 2025 winner. The uncertainty remains substantial because the most recent same-ward result favored the Liberal Democrats. Hannah Copley's 533 votes and 17.1% in 2025, together with her previous council experience, give the Greens a small upset path, but she would need a major further advance while both leading parties declined. Reform, the Conservatives, and the independent candidate have only remote winning chances given the supplied local results or absence of an electoral record.

The Conservatives have won every supplied Deddington contest from 2021 through the 2025 by-election, generally with vote shares in the mid-to-high 40s, and Eddie Fraser Reeves personally won this ward in 2022. That record, his local electoral experience, and a divided opposition make him the clear favorite. Labour is the strongest challenger: Annette Murphy increased her ward share from 22.4% in 2022 to 33.2% in 2024 and came within 303 votes of the Conservatives that year. However, Labour's weaker 2025 ward showing and the continued presence of Liberal Democrat and Green candidates limit confidence in an upset. The Liberal Democrats rebounded to 27.6% under a different candidate in 2025, but Hartley's own Deddington share declined across 2022–2024. Reform is highly uncertain because it has no supplied ward or candidate history; it could substantially divide the right-leaning vote and endanger the Conservative lead, but winning outright is less likely than facilitating a Labour or Liberal Democrat upset. The Greens have been very stable near 10–11%, leaving little evidence of a winning path.

This is principally a close Reform–Labour contest. Reform's Martin Andrew Topp receives a narrow edge because he defeated Labour's Ian Daniel Cardwell in the same ward in 2025, 672 votes to 619, and also won the overlapping Chorley North county contest that day. However, the 2025 margin was only 53 votes at 22% turnout, leaving substantial turnout and year-to-year uncertainty. Labour had won Chorley East decisively in every supplied regular election from 2021 through 2024, so Cardwell retains a strong chance of reversing the by-election result. The Conservative had only 160 votes in 2025, while Mark Grandi recorded about 17% in both 2023 and 2024; even a recovery to that level would be unlikely to challenge the two leaders. The Green and TUSC candidates have no supplied evidence of sufficient support to win.

This is primarily a close Labour–Reform contest. Labour has the strongest direct ward record, winning Moorland in 2022, 2023 and 2024; in 2024 it led the Conservatives 667–479 while Reform received 155. That makes Labour highly competitive even with some subsequent erosion. Reform's Liam Kelly, however, provides the most important newer signal: he won the nearby 2025 county contest with 40.71%, demonstrating both substantial recent Reform support and a stronger personal record than Reform's 2024 Moorland candidate. That is enough to make Kelly a very narrow favorite, but not decisively so because the packet contains no 2025 Moorland result and Labour's repeated ward victories are more directly applicable. Conservative Matthew Fido retains a meaningful upset path through his prior Moorland win, local profile and 32.7% share in 2024, though a strong Reform candidacy is likely to constrain Conservative recovery. The Greens and Liberal Democrats have historically polled far below winning level here and have only remote chances under FPTP.

This is essentially a toss-up between Conservative David Linghorn-Baker and Liberal Democrat John Loxley. Conservatives won every supplied Prettygate election from 2021 through 2024 and led by 606 votes in 2024, supporting a narrow Conservative edge. However, Loxley is an established local challenger who came within 22 votes in 2023, while the 2026 Conservative is not one of the ward's prior winners. Reform's addition creates further uncertainty and may fragment the vote that previously sustained Conservative pluralities; its candidate recorded 12% elsewhere in Colchester in 2024. Labour and Green previously polled only about 13% and 4% respectively in Prettygate, leaving them very unlikely to win without an extraordinary realignment.

Anthony John Payne is the clear favorite because he won this exact ward for Reform UK in May 2025, defeating Labour by 789 votes to 630, and now benefits from being the returning winning candidate. His separate 26.8% result in Barnstaple South also suggests that the ward victory was not wholly idiosyncratic. Labour remains the only substantial challenger: it dominated the ward through 2023 and narrowly retained it in 2024, so a partial recovery from its 2025 defeat is plausible. However, Labour's local vote declined markedly from 1,246 in 2022 to 630 in 2025, while its 2026 candidate has no demonstrated electoral strength in the supplied record. The Conservatives, Greens, and Liberal Democrats begin far behind based on recent same-ward results. Martin Ayres and Michael Geoffrey Payne have also previously posted modest shares here, making a win unlikely absent an exceptional collapse by both leading parties.

Amanda Elizabeth Napper is the clear favorite because she personally won the same ward for Reform UK in May 2025, taking 58.26% and defeating Labour by 889 votes to 486. That result is highly relevant and only one year old. However, uncertainty remains substantial: the 2025 contest was a low-turnout by-election, while Labour won Throston in each regular election from 2022 through 2024 and was particularly strong in 2024. Labour is therefore the credible alternative if its prior support reasserts itself. The Conservative candidate has recorded only modest shares in other Hartlepool wards, while the Liberal Democrat and Green candidates have no supplied electoral records; all three would require an unusually large realignment to win.

Labour is the overwhelming favorite. It won every supplied ward election from 2021 through 2024 with roughly 61%–65% of the vote, while no opponent exceeded about 24%, and the 2024 opposition was fragmented among Conservatives, Reform, Greens, Liberal Democrats, and the SDP. John Tudor also previously won this exact ward with 61.93% in 2022, providing both incumbency and direct electoral evidence. Reform and the Conservatives have the most plausible upset paths: they were nearly level in the ward in 2024, and their current candidates respectively have a recent 10.3% result elsewhere in Leeds and an 11.55% result in this ward. The Green candidate has a local profile, but the party's recent ward baseline was only around 10%; Liberal Democrat and SDP records are substantially weaker. A small upset probability remains because local turnout is low and party support can shift sharply, but overturning Labour's persistent margin would require an exceptional collapse combined with opposition consolidation.

Labour is a narrow favorite rather than a confident one. It won every supplied Bowthorpe contest from 2021 through 2025 and Mike Sands previously won the ward in 2022, but the decisive recent evidence is the 2025 by-election, where Labour defeated Nick Taylor of Reform UK by just one vote, 689–688. Taylor's repeat candidacy gives Reform a substantial chance of converting that near miss into a win. Labour's longer-running ward strength and Sands's apparent local electoral experience provide its modest edge. The Green Party's 430 votes in 2025 make it the most plausible upset alternative, although it remained well behind the leading pair and has changed candidates. The Conservatives have declined from second place in earlier elections and finished fourth in 2025, while recent Liberal Democrat support has been very low.

Sajjad Ahmed is the narrow favorite because he is the sitting councillor elected in this exact ward in 2022, and the ward's 2024 result shows that a former Labour figure can retain very strong support while running as an Independent. Labour nonetheless has a substantial chance: it won here in 2021–2023, and Ahmed's 2022 victory was close enough to suggest that some of his support came from the party label rather than a purely personal vote. The principal uncertainty is how the historically Labour-leaning electorate divides between Ahmed and Labour newcomer Adam Thomas Walsh. Reform has a meaningful upset path if that division is close, supported by John Metcalfe's 32.38% showing in a 2025 Lancashire contest, though it was in a different area. The Conservatives are disadvantaged by Reform's presence and Pauline McCormick's repeated 16–18% results in a neighboring ward. The Liberal Democrats and Greens have only remote winning chances given their candidates' consistently low prior vote shares.

Labour is favored because it won every supplied ward election from 2021 through 2024, received roughly 52–59% each time, and retained a very large lead in 2024. Mary Aspinall also has the strongest candidate-specific record, having won this ward with 57.1% in 2022, and now benefits from incumbency. Considerable uncertainty remains because the most recent ward result is two years old and the Green candidate's current statement claims Labour is falling while Green and Reform are surging. That claim is interested and unsupported by independent data in the packet, so it does not outweigh Labour's history, but it raises the upset risk materially. Green is rated the likelier challenger because Driver presents a detailed, ward-specific campaign and claims a close Green–Reform contest. Reform also has a plausible path given its 2024 presence and Bunker's prior 15% elsewhere in Plymouth, although his supplied statement is aimed at Devonport rather than Sutton and Mount Gould. The Conservatives, Liberal Democrats, and TUSC have shown too little recent ward support to have more than a remote winning chance.

Labour's Tom Coles is the narrow favorite because he is the sitting councillor elected here in 2022, when he received 1,214 votes and 43.48%, and candidate-specific incumbency may improve on Labour's weaker 2023–24 results. The Liberal Democrats are highly competitive: they won Fratton in 2021, 2023 and 2024, have maintained a substantial local vote, and Billy Ansell's statement indicates an active ward campaign backed by existing councillors. Ansell is nevertheless a first-time candidate replacing an established Liberal Democrat incumbent. Jacob Lee Short is a serious third contender after rising from 457 votes in 2023 to 729 in 2024, only 95 votes behind the winner. His move from the Portsmouth Independent Party to Reform UK creates unusually wide uncertainty because his demonstrated personal/local vote may not transfer perfectly, while the new label could also attract different voters; his supplied statement still promotes the former independent positioning. The Conservatives and Greens have recently polled only single-digit shares in Fratton and lack a plausible path to first place absent an extreme realignment. With no ward polling or 2025 result supplied and three credible leading candidates, confidence in any one winner remains limited.

Labour's Angela Mary Thompson is the narrow favorite. Labour won New Bilton in every supplied election from 2021 through 2025, and Thompson herself won the 2025 by-election. However, that latest contest was close: Labour received 466 votes to Reform's 410, far less decisive than Labour's earlier victories. Reform therefore has a substantial upset chance, strengthened by Anne-Marie Sonko's own 2025 county-council victory. The Conservatives have historically placed second in the ward but fell to a distant third in 2025, while recent Green and Liberal Democrat support has been too low to offer more than a remote path to victory. Low turnout and the sharp change between 2024 and 2025 warrant considerable uncertainty between Labour and Reform.

Labour is the overwhelming favorite. It won every supplied Eccles contest, taking 60.17% with Nathaniel Djangmah Tetteh in 2022 and polling above 2,000 votes in both 2023 and 2024, versus no more than 565 for any opponent in 2024. Tetteh also benefits from incumbency and an established local profile. Defeat would require an unusually large collapse or consolidation behind a challenger, while the six-candidate field instead risks splitting the opposition. Reform UK is assigned the largest non-Labour chance because its new entry creates the packet's main source of uncertainty, although it has no candidate or ward record here. The Conservatives have historically been Labour's nearest opponent, while the Greens have a locally connected candidate but explicitly indicate limited campaign resources. Previous Liberal Democrat and TUSC performances were too small to make victory more than a remote possibility.

Labour incumbent Minesh Parekh is the favorite: Labour won three of the four supplied ward contests, Parekh personally won here in 2022, and Labour recorded a large 2024 lead. However, the 2021–2023 Labour–Liberal Democrat margins were all narrow, including a Liberal Democrat victory in 2023, so Jordan Barry remains a substantial challenger after taking 31.93% here in 2024. The Green candidate has demonstrated campaign strength with 32.07% in Walkley, but Greens received only about 14–19% in the supplied Crookes & Crosspool elections, making victory possible but distinctly less likely than a Labour or Liberal Democrat win. Conservative and TUSC support has recently been very low in the ward. Reform is untested here and may gain votes, but the packet provides little basis for a seat-winning surge.

Labour's Asma Saeed Suleman is favored because she won this ward in 2025 and Labour also won it by large margins in every regular election from 2021 through 2024. However, the 2025 result was close: Labour led the Liberal Democrats by only 41 votes, 33.2% to 30.8%, so the Liberal Democrat candidate retains a substantial upset chance despite having no supplied electoral record. Reform starts from a meaningful 17% ward showing in 2025, but would require a much larger swing to pass both leading parties. The Conservatives, Greens, and TUSC were far behind in the most recent same-ward contest. Uncertainty remains elevated because the latest result differed sharply from earlier Labour landslides and the packet contains no 2026 polling or other direct measure of current support.

Liberal Democrat Duncan Merlin Jones is the favorite because the party won Peartree in every supplied election from 2021 through 2025, and Jones himself won convincingly in 2022 with 51.78%. His incumbency, local profile, and council experience should provide some candidate-level advantage. However, the Liberal Democrat margin narrowed substantially in the 2025 by-election, when Reform finished only 45 votes behind, making Reform the clear principal challenger despite its new candidate having no supplied electoral record. Labour was competitive from 2021 through 2024 but fell to 19.8% in 2025, while its current candidate's only supplied prior result was a modest 13.54% in a county division; it retains a smaller path in a volatile three-way contest. The Greens have remained around 7–8%, and the Conservatives fell to 8% in 2025, so either winning would require an exceptional realignment. Low historical turnout and the sharp 2025 change warrant substantial uncertainty rather than treating the Liberal Democrat winning streak as decisive.

Malcolm Wallace is the overwhelming favorite. Greens won Central Meon Valley in each election from 2022 through 2024, with vote shares of about 55%, 63%, and 51%, and margins over the Conservatives of 752, 1,279, and 757 votes. Wallace personally won the ward in 2022 and subsequently won the broader Meon Valley county by-election in 2024, providing evidence of both incumbency and a strong personal vote. David Newberry is the most plausible alternative because the Conservatives were the ward's previous winners and have consistently finished second, but reversing the recent margins would require a major swing; Reform's presence may further divide the non-Green right. Roger Bentote has relevant local council experience, but recent Liberal Democrat ward results have been far below the Greens. Reform has no supplied local record, while Labour's recent support in the ward has been minimal. Residual uncertainty allows for an unusual campaign or turnout shock, but the historical evidence strongly favors Wallace.

Labour is the clear, though not certain, favourite based on Buckingham’s most recent contest: it won in 2024 with 681 votes (51.1%), ahead of Conservative Kevin Boram on 424 (31.8%). That represented a substantial reversal from Conservative wins in 2021 and 2022, so the ward has demonstrated volatility rather than a fixed Labour baseline. Boram’s prior wins in Buckingham and his county-council experience make the Conservatives the principal conventional alternative, but his own 2024 defeat is adverse evidence. A new Reform candidate creates an additional route for a non-Labour upset, particularly by changing the division of the anti-Labour vote, but there is no local electoral record to establish a strong standalone base. Green candidate Gabrielle Crisp has relevant elected experience in Adur and is therefore given a meaningful outside chance, though the Green vote in Buckingham was only 70 in 2024. The Liberal Democrat candidate’s prior 3.05% result in another Adur ward and the party’s declining Buckingham vote indicate a low win chance. The absence of an incumbent Labour candidate and the expanded five-way field keep Labour’s probability well below certainty.

Labour is favoured because it won the ward’s most recent three single-seat contests (2022–24), including in 2024 despite a close Conservative challenge. Cait Fuller has also previously polled 28.89% for Labour in a nearby ward, indicating some candidate-level electoral experience. The principal uncertainty is Abigail Compton-Burnett: she was elected here in the 2021 multi-member contest and came within 16 votes of Labour in 2024, though she now stands as an Independent rather than Conservative. That candidacy, alongside Reform UK, is likely to fragment the non-Labour vote. Mike Patchett has prior local visibility but no win and faces both the former Conservative incumbent and Reform for similar voters. Green and Liberal Democrat historical ward support has generally been low, leaving them as remote winning possibilities.

This is chiefly a Labour–Liberal Democrat contest, with a meaningful Green upside. Labour won every regular West Chesterton contest supplied from 2021 through 2024, and Richard Swift personally won in 2022; that gives Labour a strong underlying ward record and a candidate-recognition advantage. However, the most recent and directly comparable single-seat contest, the 2025 by-election, was won by the Liberal Democrats 1,204 to Labour's 1,030. The Liberal Democrats had also consistently been second in the preceding regular elections, generally within a few hundred votes of Labour. With a new Liberal Democrat candidate, the 2025 result is strong party evidence but less decisive than it would be with the same candidate, leaving Labour narrowly favoured rather than clearly so. Hannah Copley has demonstrated both local elected experience and a substantial 2025 West Chesterton vote, so Green is the principal potential disruptor, though still materially behind the top two on the available results. Reform received 197 votes in 2025 and is more plausible than the Conservatives or an unknown independent, but has little evidence of a path to first place. The full six-candidate field and different turnout/context between regular elections and the by-election warrant substantial uncertainty.

Conservatives have won every listed Deddington contest from 2021 through the 2025 by-election, with 2025 especially strong: 1,560 votes versus 916 for the Liberal Democrats, 478 for Labour, and 370 for the Greens. Eddie Fraser Reeves also won this ward in 2022 with 45.9%, giving the Conservative candidate a relevant prior ward victory, though his 2025 county result elsewhere was weaker. The main uncertainty is the newly present Reform UK candidate, for whom there is no electoral record; this creates a plausible route for a fragmented non-left/right vote to reduce the Conservative lead. Labour's Annette Murphy had a strong upward trajectory through 2024, reaching 33.16%, but the 2025 by-election Labour result was much lower with a different candidate. The Liberal Democrats were second in 2025, yet James Hartley's own ward share declined materially from 20.75% in 2022 to 12.58% in 2024. Thus the Conservatives remain clear but far from certain favourites, with Liberal Democrat and Labour upsets both credible under a sufficiently large Conservative/Reform split.

Reform UK's Martin Andrew Topp is the modest favourite because he won this ward's May 2025 by-election with 40.1% (672 votes), ahead of Labour's Ian Daniel Cardwell on 36.9% (619), and both candidates return for this contest. Topp also won the contemporaneous Chorley North county division for Reform with 41.1%, supporting the view that his 2025 result was not solely an isolated ward-level outcome. However, the 53-vote Chorley East margin was narrow and Labour had won every regular ward election from 2021 through 2024 by very large margins. That leaves Labour with a substantial chance of regaining the seat if the 2025 Reform surge recedes or Labour turnout/recovery is stronger. The Conservatives were already weak in the ward before Reform's arrival and are likely to be further squeezed; Green and TUSC candidacies can affect the leading margin but have little direct winning evidence.

Labour is the modest favourite on the ward’s recent electoral record: it won Moorland in each of 2022, 2023, and 2024, with a 188-vote lead over Conservative Matthew Fido in 2024. Fido’s prior 2021 win and incumbent-style local profile make a Conservative recovery plausible, but his vote fell substantially in his 2024 re-election attempt. The principal uncertainty is Reform: it already took 155 votes in Moorland in 2024, while Liam Kelly subsequently won a Lincolnshire county seat for Reform with 40.71%, giving Reform a credible route to consolidate anti-Conservative support and overtake Labour in a fragmented contest. The Labour candidate’s own past results outside the ward are weak, so the probability is not based on candidate-specific strength. Green and Liberal Democrat prior local support exists but is too low, absent stronger ward evidence, to make either likely to win.

This is principally a Conservative–Liberal Democrat contest. Prettygate has returned Conservatives at every listed election from 2021 through 2024, including a 606-vote Conservative lead in 2024. However, John Loxley is a notably persistent local Liberal Democrat candidate: his vote rose from 544 in 2021 to 1,050 in 2022 and 1,279 in 2023, when he lost by only 22 votes. His 2024 result was weaker, but he remains the clear alternative candidate and has a long local-campaigning profile.

David Linghorn-Baker lacks a prior Prettygate result but presents a substantial locally focused campaign, states that he works in the ward, and reports five years of activity alongside the existing Conservative councillors. That, combined with the ward's repeated Conservative victories and prior candidate incumbency/local-organisation effects, leaves the Conservatives narrow favourites. The absence of the previous winning Conservative candidates and the close 2023 contest keep the Liberal Democrats very much competitive.

Reform UK could draw some support, particularly from voters otherwise inclined to Conservatives, but its candidate's recorded results elsewhere were modest and there is no ward-specific record. Labour's 12.97% in 2024 establishes a meaningful but not winning base; the Green candidate has no supplied electoral record and the party's prior Prettygate results were low. These parties create additional uncertainty around the leading pair but are unlikely winners on the supplied evidence.

Reform UK's Anthony John Payne is the clear, but not overwhelming, favourite: he won the same ward's May 2025 by-election with 789 votes (34.83%), ahead of Labour on 630, and is defending that seat personally only a year later. Labour remains the principal challenger because it won every regular ward contest from 2021 through 2024, including 842 votes in 2024, and some of the 259 votes cast for the 2025 independent candidate may be available to other parties now that no independent is standing. The 2025 result nevertheless provides the most direct evidence for this single-seat contest and gives Payne an incumbent and candidate-recognition advantage. Labour's current candidate has limited support in the listed prior contests outside the ward, which modestly weakens its recovery case. Green support was about 11% in both relevant 2025 contests, while Conservatives and Liberal Democrats have recent ward bases but were well behind the top two in the by-election. The material uncertainty is chiefly whether Labour's established ward strength and the absent independent vote can overturn Reform's recent lead.

Amanda Elizabeth Napper is the clear favourite: she is the sitting Reform councillor, having won the Throston by-election only a year earlier with 889 votes (58.26%), substantially ahead of Labour’s 486 and the Conservatives’ 151. Her local record therefore supplies unusually direct, recent evidence of both candidate and party strength in this ward. Labour remains the principal alternative because it won the regular ward contests in 2022, 2023, and especially 2024, when it recorded 1,015 votes; a regular-election electorate and a different Labour candidate could produce meaningful reversion from the low-turnout by-election result. The new Liberal Democrat and Green candidacies add vote-splitting uncertainty, chiefly on the non-Reform side, but neither has ward-specific candidate evidence. The Conservative candidate has prior local candidacies but modest recorded shares and the party was a distant third in the 2025 by-election.

Labour is the clear baseline favourite. It won Killingbeck & Seacroft in every provided 2021–24 contest, with 2,446–2,946 votes and roughly three-fifths or more of the vote each time. John Tudor was himself elected for Labour in the ward in 2022 with 61.93%, giving the Labour candidacy an additional local-record advantage. The 2024 result is especially emphatic: Labour's 2,551 votes exceeded the combined total of every other candidate.

There is nevertheless meaningful uncertainty because the contest is two years after the latest ward result and Reform appeared locally in 2024 with 435 votes, close to the Conservative total of 481. David Dresser has prior Reform candidacy experience in Leeds, so Reform is the principal non-Labour route to an upset if its support grows sharply and Labour support falls. The Conservatives retain a smaller possibility, but their ward vote fell markedly across the supplied history and their 2024 candidate received only 11.55%. Green support has been persistent but far below a winning level, while Liberal Democrat and SDP histories indicate very low local ceilings. No polling, intervening same-ward results, or campaign evidence is supplied to justify treating any challenger as close to Labour.

This is principally a Labour–Reform contest. The May 2025 Bowthorpe by-election was effectively tied: Labour won by one vote (689 to 688), with Nick Taylor himself the Reform candidate. That very recent, strong Reform performance makes Taylor highly competitive and rules out treating Labour's much larger 2021–24 margins as fully predictive. However, Labour retain a small edge: they won the 2025 contest, won every regular Bowthorpe election from 2021 through 2024 by substantial margins, and their 2026 candidate Mike Sands previously won in Bowthorpe in 2022. The Green Party's 430 votes in 2025 establishes a meaningful but still clearly third-place base; it has an outside chance if the two leading parties split or fall back. The Conservatives were only fourth in 2025 and their prior Bowthorpe support has declined markedly, while the Liberal Democrats have consistently had a small local vote, leaving both as very unlikely winners.

The contest is principally between Independent Sajjad Ahmed and Labour's Adam Thomas Walsh. Ahmed won this ward for Labour in 2022 with 1,579 votes (48.27%), giving him a meaningful personal electoral record, while the ward also elected an Independent by a very large margin in 2024. Those results indicate that an established local candidate can be highly competitive outside the Labour label. Labour nevertheless has a deep recent base: it won each of the 2021–23 ward contests, often decisively, so its new candidate remains very capable of consolidating enough of that vote to win. Reform is the principal third-party upset route, supported by Metcalfe's 32.38% county result in 2025, but that was in another division and there is no equivalent ward-specific Reform evidence. Conservatives were competitive in 2021–22 but collapsed in the two subsequent ward contests; Liberal Democrat and Green records suggest little winning potential. The Labour–Independent split and FPTP make this a genuinely uncertain race rather than a safe hold.

Labour is the clear base-rate favourite. It won Sutton and Mount Gould in each of 2021–24, with 51.5% to 58.7% of the vote and broadly stable raw totals of about 1,700–1,785 votes despite declining turnout. Mary Aspinall additionally has a prior ward victory in 2022, providing a meaningful incumbency and name-recognition advantage. In 2024 Labour still took 56.4%, while the largest opponent had only 14.9%; the opposition was divided among Conservatives, Greens, Reform, Liberal Democrats and TUSC.

The forecast nevertheless allows substantial risk of a changed contest. The Conservative vote has fallen sharply across the ward history, while Green support rose to 11.4% in 2024 and Reform entered with 8.4%. Reform's candidate previously won 15.0% in nearby Devonport, and the Green candidate presents evidence of local activity. The Green statement's assertion that Labour is collapsing and that Green and Reform are close is relevant as a contemporaneous campaign signal, but is an unverified, interested-party claim and conflicts with the stable Labour ward results through 2024; it should not outweigh the observed record. Reform and Green therefore have the principal upset chances, with Reform marginally ahead because it may draw from the previously declining Conservative bloc. Conservatives retain a smaller residual path, while Liberal Democrat and TUSC historical support gives them only remote winning chances.

Labour’s Tom Coles has the strongest individual incumbency signal: he won this ward for Labour in 2022 with 43.5%, and this is a close, competitive ward where candidate recognition can matter substantially. The Liberal Democrats have won three of the four supplied ward contests, including the narrow 2024 result, and their candidate presents a local campaigning and councillor-team case, making them the principal alternative. Jacob Lee Short was only just behind the 2024 winner and has a demonstrated personal local vote, but the move from Portsmouth Independent Party to Reform UK creates material uncertainty about how much of that support transfers. The Conservatives and Greens have recently been well behind the leading contenders. The recent volatility and three plausible routes to a plurality warrant meaningful uncertainty rather than a decisive Labour forecast.

Labour is narrowly favoured because Angela Mary Thompson is the sitting ward councillor and won the 2025 New Bilton by-election, while Labour won each regular ward contest from 2021 through 2024 by large margins. However, the 2025 result was much closer than those earlier contests: Reform polled 410 votes to Labour's 466, with the Conservatives, Greens and Liberal Democrats also standing. Reform therefore has a credible path if it gains modestly or consolidates anti-Labour votes. Anne-Marie Sonko's 2025 county-council win with 33.71% provides some evidence of candidate and party strength, though it was in another division and cannot be assumed to transfer fully. The Conservative candidate has no listed record and the smaller-party candidates' prior local results are low, making them outside chances under FPTP.

Labour is the overwhelming favourite. Eccles has returned Labour in every supplied election, with Labour’s single-seat vote totals of 1,964 (2022), 2,022 (2023), and 2,121 (2024), while the strongest non-Labour party in each case was far behind. Nathaniel Djangmah Tetteh is also the 2022 winner seeking re-election and presents a detailed local-incumbency record, adding a candidate-specific advantage. The main uncertainty is that the 2026 field includes a new Reform UK candidate, who could attract votes not represented in the recent ward contests, while Conservative support has historically been the largest established opposition bloc. But their likely competition for overlapping anti-Labour votes, combined with Labour’s very large recent margins, makes an upset unlikely. Green support has been meaningful but well short of contention and the candidate explicitly notes limited campaigning; Liberal Democrat and TUSC records are weaker still.

Labour is favoured because it won Crookes & Crosspool in 2021, 2022, and decisively in 2024; its candidate, Minesh Parekh, was personally elected here in 2022. The 2024 Labour lead over Liberal Democrat Jordan Barry was 1,122 votes (3,310 to 2,188), giving the incumbent party a meaningful baseline advantage. However, this is not a safe enough history to imply near-certainty: Labour's 2021 and 2022 wins were narrow, while the Liberal Democrats won the 2023 contest by 187 votes. Barry's prior 31.93% in this ward makes the Liberal Democrats the principal alternative. The Greens have consistently polled material but third-place shares locally, although Dylan Lewis-Creser's 32.07% in Walkley in 2024 supports a non-trivial upside case. Conservatives and TUSC have recently been far behind locally; Reform has no supplied local record, so each is a remote winner scenario rather than a central contender.

Labour is the favourite because Holywell returned Labour comfortably in each regular election from 2021 through 2024, and Asma Saeed Suleman personally won the 2025 by-election. However, that by-election was much closer than the earlier contests: Labour received 573 votes (33.16%) against 532 for the Liberal Democrats (30.79%), while Reform, Conservative, Green, and TUSC candidates divided the remainder. This establishes a plausible Liberal Democrat path if Labour support softens or tactical voting consolidates, but the Liberal Democrat candidate has no supplied electoral record whereas Labour fields the sitting by-election winner. Reform's 2025 294 votes make it a meaningful source of uncertainty and a remote winner possibility, though it would need a substantial further advance to overtake both Labour and the Liberal Democrats. Conservative support has declined sharply in the ward, while Green and TUSC have limited prior Holywell support.

Liberal Democrat Duncan Merlin Jones is the clear favourite. Peartree returned Liberal Democrats in each regular election from 2021 through 2024, and Jones personally won the 2022 contest with 51.78%. He is an incumbent local councillor with a detailed local record and campaign statement, an advantage not held by the other principal candidates. The key warning is the 2025 by-election: the Liberal Democrats won only narrowly, 420 votes to Reform UK's 375, while Labour fell back and Conservatives were weak. That makes Reform the principal upset route, particularly if its 2025 support persists. However, Jones's personal incumbency and the Liberal Democrats' longer ward record make a repeat Liberal Democrat win more likely than not by a substantial margin. Labour has some historical second-place strength but its listed candidate's recent county-election performance was modest; Green and Conservative victory paths require much larger shifts than shown in the ward results.

Malcolm Wallace is the overwhelming favourite. The Greens have won each of the three subsequent annual elections in Central Meon Valley after 2021, with Wallace himself winning decisively in 2022 (1,955 votes to 1,203 Conservative). Green leads subsequently widened substantially: 2,168 to 889 in 2023 and 1,717 to 960 in 2024. Wallace is also an established local Green councillor and a 2024 Hampshire county-election winner, giving him unusually strong candidate recognition and organisation for this contest. The Conservative vote remains the most plausible alternative base, but now faces a Reform candidate as well as the continued Liberal Democrat presence. The Liberal Democrat candidate has relevant neighbouring-area council experience, which creates a modest possibility of a stronger-than-usual Liberal Democrat performance, but the ward’s repeated Green victories make an outright upset unlikely. Labour has consistently been marginal locally.
